The City\'s total direct capital commitment (infrastructure, land write-down, and permit fee waivers) exceeds **\$23 million**, supplemented by TIF reimbursement of up to \$48 million funded by the project\'s own tax increment.

The return on this public investment is substantial. Lakemont Station Commons will generate an expanded and diversified tax base upon TIF district expiration, create an estimated 1,800 construction jobs and 900 permanent jobs, deliver 106 units of affordable housing, remediate a brownfield site, and provide lasting public amenities including a community center, public plaza, and public parking facility. The project will transform a vacant, contaminated former industrial campus into one of the most significant transit-oriented developments in the Chicago metropolitan region.

**Infrastructure Cost-Sharing.** The total infrastructure budget for the project is **\$20,800,000**, comprising \$12,500,000 in City-funded off-site improvements and \$8,300,000 in Developer-funded on-site improvements. The City\'s off-site investment includes road widening of Lakemont Station Boulevard, a new traffic signal at the Oak Street intersection, water main extension, and sanitary sewer upgrade. The Developer\'s on-site investment includes internal roadways, sidewalks, stormwater management, utility connections, and site lighting. This cost-sharing model reflects the shared public-private nature of the investment and ensures that both on-site and off-site infrastructure are constructed to the standards necessary to support the project\'s density and the broader community.

**[7.4 Tax Increment Financing Analysis]{.underline}**

The project site is located within the **Lakemont Central TIF District**, established by Ordinance No. 2019-42, with a statutory expiration date of **December 31, 2042**. The TIF District encompasses the project site and surrounding parcels, and was established to facilitate the redevelopment of the former Lakemont Ironworks campus and adjacent underutilized properties.

The Developer anticipates TIF reimbursement of up to **\$48,000,000** for eligible costs, which include public infrastructure improvements, environmental remediation expenditures, parking structure construction, and other qualifying expenditures under the TIF Act (65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-1 et seq.) and the TIF District\'s Redevelopment Plan. TIF reimbursement will be administered on a **pay-as-you-go basis**: the Developer will fund eligible expenditures upfront and submit annual reimbursement requests from the tax increment generated by the project\'s increased assessed value above the frozen base.

The Developer\'s incremental TIF projection is as follows:

> **Phase I Completion (approximately 2028):** Upon completion and assessment of the Phase I improvements (320 residential units, 45,000 sq ft retail, 480-space parking), partial tax increment will begin to flow to the TIF fund. The Developer estimates initial annual increment in the range of **\$1,500,000 to \$2,000,000** per year, reflecting the Phase I assessed value and the Phase I property tax abatement (which reduces but does not eliminate the TIF-eligible increment).
>
> **Phase II Completion (approximately 2031):** The addition of 280,000 sq ft of Class A office, 18,000 sq ft of retail, and 350-space parking will significantly increase the project\'s assessed value and annual increment. The Developer estimates annual increment will grow to approximately **\$2,800,000 to \$3,200,000** per year upon Phase II stabilization.
>
> **Phase III and Phase IV Completion (approximately 2034--2035):** Full buildout of all project components --- including Residential Tower B, the hotel, the community center, public plaza, and public parking structure --- will bring the annual TIF increment to an estimated **\$4,200,000 per year** at full assessment.
>
> **Cumulative Increment through TIF Expiration (December 31, 2042):** Based on the above projections, the Developer estimates that total cumulative TIF reimbursement over the life of the district will be in the range of **\$40,000,000 to \$48,000,000**, which approaches or reaches the \$48 million cap.

This projection assumes that phases are completed on or ahead of the target accelerated schedule, assessment appeals are resolved in a timely manner, and equalized assessed values reflect the anticipated improvements. The Developer will work with the City to monitor annual increment generation and coordinate reimbursement requests to maximize the project\'s TIF benefit within the district\'s remaining life.

**[Section 8: Infrastructure Plan]{.underline}**

**[8.1 On-Site Infrastructure]{.underline}**

The Developer will be responsible for the design and construction of **\$8,300,000** in on-site infrastructure improvements necessary to support the Lakemont Station Commons development. On-site infrastructure will be constructed primarily during Phase I, with supplemental connections and extensions for subsequent phases as each phase commences.

On-site infrastructure improvements include:

> • Internal roadway network providing vehicular circulation throughout the development, including service and emergency access routes
>
> • Sidewalks, pedestrian pathways, and bicycle routes connecting all project buildings to the public plaza, Metra station, and Lakemont Station Boulevard
>
> • Site grading and earthwork to establish finished grades for all building pads, roadways, and the public plaza
>
> • Stormwater management systems, including on-site detention, bioswales, permeable paving areas, and rain gardens, designed to meet or exceed local and IEPA stormwater management requirements
>
> • Utility connections for water service, sanitary sewer, electrical power, natural gas, and telecommunications, extending from the public right-of-way or utility mains to each building location
>
> • Site lighting for internal roadways, pedestrian paths, parking areas, and the public plaza, utilizing energy-efficient LED fixtures with dark-sky-compliant design
>
> • Landscaping within the development parcels, including street trees, ornamental plantings, and green infrastructure elements consistent with the LEED Gold design standards

The Developer will coordinate on-site infrastructure construction with the City\'s off-site improvement schedule to ensure seamless integration of utility connections, roadway alignments, and stormwater management systems.

**[8.2 Off-Site Infrastructure (City-Funded)]{.underline}**

The City of Lakemont has committed **\$12,500,000** toward off-site infrastructure improvements that are critical prerequisites for the Lakemont Station Commons development. These improvements will be designed and constructed by or on behalf of the City, with the Developer providing coordination and input to ensure compatibility with the on-site development program.

Off-site infrastructure improvements include:

> • **Road Widening of Lakemont Station Boulevard:** Widening of the existing two-lane roadway to a four-lane cross-section with a center turn lane, curb-and-gutter improvements, and dedicated bicycle lanes, extending from the project site\'s eastern boundary to the intersection with Main Street. This improvement is essential to accommodate the traffic generated by the project\'s 1,620,000 square feet of development and 1,430 parking spaces.
>
> • **New Traffic Signal at Oak Street Intersection:** Installation of a fully actuated traffic signal at the intersection of Lakemont Station Boulevard and Oak Street, located at the project\'s primary vehicular entrance. The traffic signal is required to manage turning movements and ensure safe pedestrian crossings between the project site and the Metra station.
>
> • **Water Main Extension:** Extension of a 16-inch water main from the existing trunk line on Main Street along Lakemont Station Boulevard to serve the project site, providing adequate domestic water supply and fire flow capacity for the full buildout.
>
> • **Sanitary Sewer Upgrade:** Upgrade of the existing 12-inch sanitary sewer line along Lakemont Station Boulevard to a 24-inch line, with a new connection to the regional interceptor, to accommodate the wastewater flows generated by the project\'s residential, commercial, and hotel uses.

The combined on-site and off-site infrastructure budget totals **\$20,800,000** (\$8,300,000 Developer + \$12,500,000 City). Off-site improvements are critical to Phase I and must be completed on a timeline coordinated with the Phase I construction schedule. The Developer will work closely with the City\'s Community Development Department and Public Works Department to develop a construction sequencing plan that minimizes disruption and ensures that utility connections and roadway improvements are available when needed for Phase I occupancy.

**[Section 9: Compliance and Regulatory Considerations]{.underline}**

**[9.1 Prevailing Wage]{.underline}**

The Developer acknowledges that construction within the Lakemont Central TIF District is subject to applicable provisions of the **Illinois Prevailing Wage Act** (820 ILCS 130/). The Developer commits to compliance with prevailing wage requirements for all construction activities associated with the Lakemont Station Commons project. The Developer will require all contractors and subcontractors performing work on the project to comply with the prevailing wage rates established by the Illinois Department of Labor for Lake County, and will include prevailing wage compliance provisions in all construction contracts. The Developer will maintain certified payroll records and submit them to the City on a periodic basis as required by law and the development agreement.

**[9.2 Zoning and Entitlements]{.underline}**

As described in Section 3.3, the project site is currently zoned M-2 (General Manufacturing), which is incompatible with the proposed mixed-use program. The Developer will cooperate with the City Community Development Department to pursue rezoning to **PD-TOD (Planned Development--Transit Oriented District)**. The PD-TOD classification is expected to accommodate the proposed density, building heights, use mix, and design standards. Rezoning must be completed and approved by the City Council prior to execution of the development agreement. The Developer anticipates filing the rezoning application promptly upon selection as the preferred developer.

**[9.3 Environmental Compliance]{.underline}**

As detailed in Section 3.2, the Developer will enroll Parcel 12-24-300-016 in the **IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program (VSRP)** and prepare a Remedial Action Plan (RAP) for IEPA review and approval. A **No Further Remediation (NFR) letter** must be obtained from IEPA prior to residential development on Parcel 016. The Developer anticipates that IEPA approval of the remediation plan will be a condition precedent to land conveyance, consistent with the City Council\'s anticipated requirements. All environmental remediation activities will be conducted in full compliance with applicable IEPA regulations and the terms of the VSRP enrollment.

**[Section 10: Project Schedule Summary and Key Milestones]{.underline}**

The following table consolidates all key project milestones, presenting both the Developer\'s target accelerated schedule and the contractual maximum deadlines that would govern under the development agreement:

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Milestone**                                         **Target Date**                     **Contractual Deadline**
  ----------------------------------------------------- ----------------------------------- ----------------------------------------
  Rezoning to PD-TOD                                    Q2 2025                             Prior to DA execution

  Development Agreement Execution                       June 2025                           ---

  Land Conveyance                                       Q3 2025                             Following DA execution

  Environmental Remediation Commencement (Parcel 016)   Q3 2025                             Prior to Phase I vertical construction

  Phase I Groundbreaking                                Q1 2026                             No later than March 1, 2026

  Phase I Substantial Completion                        Q3 2028                             September 30, 2028 (30 months)

  Phase II Commencement                                 Q4 2028 (target)                    Within 12 months of Phase I SC

  Phase II Substantial Completion                       Q4 2030 (target)                    24 months from Phase II commencement

  Phase III Commencement                                Q1 2031 (target)                    Within 12 months of Phase II SC

  Phase III Substantial Completion                      Q3 2033 (target)                    30 months from Phase III commencement

  Phase IV Commencement                                 Q1 2031 (concurrent w/ Phase III)   Concurrent with Phase III

  Phase IV Substantial Completion                       Q1 2033 (target)                    24 months from Phase IV commencement

  TIF District Expiration                               ---                                 December 31, 2042
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Under the contractual maximum timeline scenario (assuming each phase utilizes the full permitted commencement window and construction duration), Phase II Substantial Completion would extend to approximately September 2031, Phase III Substantial Completion to approximately March 2035, and Phase IV Substantial Completion to approximately September 2034. The Developer\'s target accelerated schedule is approximately two years ahead of these maximum dates.

The milestone table illustrates that even under the maximum contractual timeline, all phases reach Substantial Completion well in advance of the TIF District\'s December 31, 2042 expiration date, allowing multiple years of full-buildout TIF increment collection. The Developer is committed to maintaining the accelerated schedule as its primary business objective, while the contractual maximums provide the flexibility necessary to navigate a decade-long development program.

**[Section 11: Conclusion and Request for Selection]{.underline}**

Granite City Development LLC is committed to delivering **Lakemont Station Commons** as a transformative transit-oriented development that will catalyze economic growth, provide critically needed affordable housing, create construction and permanent employment opportunities, remediate a brownfield site, and deliver lasting public amenities to the Lakemont community.

The Developer\'s experienced leadership team, under the direction of Marcus J. Pellegrini, brings nearly three decades of successful Chicagoland development experience spanning mixed-use, transit-oriented, and affordable housing projects. The project is supported by a robust capital structure, including **\$85 million in committed equity** from the Pellegrini Family Trust and Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, a **\$210 million preliminary construction loan commitment** from First Prairie National Bank, and anticipated public incentives that reflect the shared public-private investment in the project\'s success.

The Lakemont Station Commons program --- 530 residential units, 280,000 square feet of Class A office, 63,000 square feet of retail, a 150-room hotel, a 2.5-acre public plaza, a 28,000 square-foot community center, and 1,430 structured parking spaces --- represents a comprehensive vision for the Lakemont Central Metra station area that exceeds the City\'s RFP requirements in scale, quality, public benefit, and sustainability commitment.

The Developer looks forward to working with **Mayor Ostrowski**, **City Manager Fong**, **Director Vasquez**, and the members of the **Lakemont City Council** to finalize the development agreement and bring this project to fruition. Lakemont Station Commons will be a source of community pride and a model for transit-oriented development in the Chicago metropolitan region.

Granite City Development LLC respectfully requests selection as the **preferred developer** for the Lakemont Central Metra station site.

Respectfully submitted,

GRANITE CITY DEVELOPMENT LLC

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Name: Marcus J. Pellegrini

Title: Manager and Chief Executive Officer

Date: December 16, 2024

**[Appendix A: Developer Entity Documentation]{.underline}**

The following entity and financial documentation is referenced in this proposal and is provided separately under **Tab A** of this submission:

> 1\. **Certificate of Organization and Certificate of Good Standing** --- Granite City Development LLC, organized under the laws of the State of Illinois on March 14, 2019, as evidenced by the Certificate of Organization filed with the Illinois Secretary of State. A current Certificate of Good Standing is included.
>
> 2\. **Operating Agreement Summary** --- Summary excerpts from the Operating Agreement of Granite City Development LLC, identifying the three members and their respective ownership interests: Pellegrini Family Trust (60%), Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP (25%), and TransitWorks Equity Group LLC (15%).
>
> 3\. **Evidence of Bonding Capacity** --- Letter from Ridgepoint Surety Corporation confirming bonding capacity of up to \$75,000,000 for the Lakemont Station Commons project.
>
> 4\. **Audited Financial Statements** --- Reference to the most recent audited financial statements of Granite City Development LLC, prepared by Clearmont Accounting Group LLP, reflecting an entity net worth of approximately \$185,000,000. Complete audited financials are available for confidential review upon request.

**[Appendix B: Preliminary Site Plan and Phasing Diagram]{.underline}**

The preliminary site plan for Lakemont Station Commons organizes the 12.7-acre site into four development zones corresponding to the four project phases:

> **Phase I** is located on the eastern portion of the site, occupying Parcel 12-24-300-015 and the eastern portions of Parcel 12-24-300-016. This location places Residential Tower A and the retail podium in the closest proximity to the Lakemont Central Metra station entrance, maximizing transit connectivity for the project\'s first residents and retail tenants.
>
> **Phase II** occupies the northern portion of the site, where the Class A office building, additional retail, and structured parking will front on Lakemont Station Boulevard. The northern positioning provides prominent commercial visibility and convenient access from the widened boulevard.
>
> **Phase III** is located on the western portion of the site, encompassing Parcel 12-24-300-017 and the remaining western portions of Parcel 016. Residential Tower B and the select-service hotel will benefit from views to the west and south, with direct pedestrian access to the central public plaza.
>
> **Phase IV** is centrally located, serving as the connective tissue between the three surrounding development phases. The 2.5-acre public plaza, 28,000 square-foot community center, and 600-space public parking structure occupy the heart of the site, creating a civic center accessible from all directions and serving as the focal point for the entire Lakemont Station Commons community.

Full architectural site plans and phasing diagrams are provided separately under **Tab B** of this submission.

**[Appendix C: Letters of Intent and Financial Commitments]{.underline}**

The following commitment letters and financial documentation are provided separately under **Tab C** of this submission:

> 1\. **Letter of Intent --- Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP.** Equity commitment of \$51,000,000, signed by Richard \"Rick\" Dominguez, Managing Partner, Lakeshore Capital Management LLC (General Partner of Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP). Dated November 22, 2024.
>
> 2\. **Letter of Commitment --- Pellegrini Family Trust.** Equity commitment of \$34,000,000, signed by Marcus J. Pellegrini, Trustee. Dated November 18, 2024.
>
> 3\. **Preliminary Construction Loan Term Sheet --- First Prairie National Bank.** Construction loan facility of \$210,000,000, subject to customary conditions including execution of development agreement, satisfactory appraisal, and construction loan due diligence. Relationship Manager: Sandra L. Chen, Senior Vice President. Dated December 2, 2024.
>
> 4\. **Bonding Letter --- Ridgepoint Surety Corporation.** Confirmation of bonding capacity of up to \$75,000,000 for performance and payment bonds in connection with the Lakemont Station Commons project. Dated November 25, 2024.

END OF PROPOSAL --- PROPRIETARY AND CONFIDENTIAL Granite City Development LLC --- RFP No. 2024-CD-07 Lakemont Station Commons

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

result:
/usr/bin/pandoc
pandoc 3.1.11.1
Features: -server +lua
Scripting engine: Lua 5.4
---VALIDATE---
"""Validate a .docx for delivery.

Checks ZIP integrity, XML well-formedness for every part, [Content_Types].xml
presence, and relationship consistency (no dangling rIds). Does NOT do XSD
schema validation in v1 — defer until ECMA-376 XSDs are vendored fresh from
ECMA-International.

Usage: python validate.py file.docx
Exit 0 = valid; non-zero = errors printed to stderr.
"""
import sys
import zipfile
from pathlib import Path
from lxml import etree


def validate(path: Path) -> list[str]:
    errors = []

    if not path.exists():
        return [f"File not found: {path}"]
    if not zipfile.is_zipfile(path):
        return [f"Not a valid ZIP: {path}"]

    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        names = set(z.namelist())

        if "[Content_Types].xml" not in names:
            errors.append("Missing [Content_Types].xml")
            return errors

        # XML well-formedness for every .xml and .rels part
        for name in names:
            if name.endswith(".xml") or name.endswith(".rels"):
                try:
                    etree.fromstring(z.read(name))
                except etree.XMLSyntaxError as e:
                    errors.append(f"Malformed XML in {name}: {e}")

        # Relationship target consistency

{"command": "cat > $WORKSPACE_DIR/notes-conflicts.md << 'EOF'
# Cross-Document Conflicts & Open Items (Developer's Perspective)

## CRITICAL CONFLICTS
1. **Reversionary interest vs. Lender prohibition** — Resolution cond.(h) requires reversionary interest (auto-revert if Phase I not SC within 36 mo of conveyance); Construction Lender PROHIBITS any defeasible fee/reversionary interest (would make loan impossible to close); Lakeshore requires consent for any clawback. → Substitute alternative mechanism.
2. **36-month reversionary window vs. Sept 30, 2028 SC deadline** — 36 months measured from conveyance (mid-2025) = ~mid-2028, BEFORE the Sept 30, 2028 SC deadline. Direct conflict.
3. **6-month Phase I commencement window vs. March 1, 2026 groundbreaking** — Term Sheet allows City termination if Phase I not commenced within 6 mo of conveyance; if conveyance mid-2025, 6 mo expires before March 1, 2026.
4. **Change-of-control thresholds** — Term Sheet 50%; Lender [50%] negotiable; Lakeshore 51%. Three different thresholds.
5. **Equity demonstration deadline** — Resolution: 60 days from Feb 18, 2025 (April 19, 2025); Term Sheet: 60 days from City Council approval of definitive Agreement (~June 2025).
6. **Affordable unit count** — Resolution/RFP: 106 (20%); Term Sheet summary table: "at least 100."
7. **Prevailing wage scope** — Term Sheet: "all construction"; Resolution: only "public works"; Corp Counsel memo: likely all, but Developer will resist private components.
8. **Living wage / first-source hiring** — Resolution cond.(c) requires $17.50/hr living wage + first-source hiring; Term Sheet SILENT.
9. **TIF shortfall risk** — Corp Counsel memo: $48M cap may fall $6-10M short (with abatement); recommends shortfall NOT be City default; TIF not general obligation. Developer fronts all costs.
10. **Abatement-TIF interaction** — Phase I abatement reduces TIF increment; internal tension.
11. **Land write-down vs. TIF cap** — Is $9.2M write-down charged against $48M TIF cap or separate? (Corp Counsel memo flags.)
12. **City infrastructure funding source** — Is $12.5M from TIF (counts against cap) or general revenue (separate)?
13. **Parcel acquisition history** — Resolution/TIF memo: Parcels 015+016 tax forfeiture 2016, 017 direct purchase 2018; ESA: Parcel 016 direct purchase 2018 (with 017). Affects title/indemnity.
14. **RFP number** — Term Sheet: 2024-CD-018; RFP narrative: 2024-CD-07.
15. **Parcel acreages** — ESA: 015=4.2, 016=4.1, 017=4.4; RFP: 015=3.8, 016=4.1, 017=4.8.
16. **Insurance requirements** — Term Sheet CGL aggregate $20M; Lender $25M; Lender adds Professional Liability $5M + Pollution $10M.
17. **Force majeure cap** — Term Sheet 18 mo aggregate; Lender only 180 days for Phase I SC default.
18. **Environmental cost overruns** — ESA recommends 25-35% contingency; who bears overruns above $3.14M? Lakeshore requires consent if >$3.14M.
19. **VSRP applicant / remediation performer** — Resolution implies Developer submits RAP; ESA says City is primary responsible party; unclear who performs/pays.
20. **NFR letter / phased conveyance** — Resolution cond.(b): RAP approval before ANY conveyance; ESA recommends phased conveyance (clean parcels first); Lender accepts RAP+escrow OR NFR.
21. **Vapor intrusion costs** — Not in $3.14M estimate; who pays?
22. **ELUC encumbrances** — Parcel 017 (and possibly 016) require recorded institutional controls = title encumbrances; must be Permitted Exceptions acceptable to Lender.
23. **Performance security dual use** — $15M bond is both City security AND Lender collateral; additional escrow/LC may be needed for clawback substitute.
24. **Community center FF&E** — "at no additional cost" (Term Sheet) vs "fully furnished and equipped" (RFP).
25. **Lender-required DA provisions** — cure rights, consent to amendments, subordination, foreclosure assignment, estoppel certificates.
26. **Additional $58.4M financing** — not secured; Lender consent required; may dilute Pellegrini Interest → change-of-control.
27. **TIF extension** — City cannot unilaterally extend; requires state legislation/new TIF (12-18 mo).
28. **GEC reliance letters** — City/Lender/Lakeshore need reliance letters to rely on ESA.
29. **Reporting frequency** — City quarterly; Lender monthly/quarterly/annual; Lakeshore monthly/quarterly/annual — harmonize.
30. **Hotel brand / architect / GC** — not yet selected; Lender approval required.
EOF
echo "Notes saved.""}

{"file_path": "draft.md", "content": "# DEVELOPMENT AGREEMENT

## LAKEMONT STATION COMMONS MIXED-USE TRANSIT-ORIENTED DEVELOPMENT

**[DRAFT — FOR DISCUSSION PURPOSES — PREPARED ON BEHALF OF GRANITE CITY DEVELOPMENT LLC]**

> **[Drafting Note — Purpose of this draft.** This Development Agreement is drafted from the perspective of Granite City Development LLC ("Developer") and reflects the principal business terms set forth in the Negotiated Term Sheet dated January 22, 2025, subject to the conditions imposed by City Council Resolution No. R-2025-017 (adopted February 18, 2025). Bracketed drafting notes throughout flag (i) conflicts among the source documents (the Resolution, the Negotiated Term Sheet, the Construction Loan Term Sheet from First Prairie National Bank, the Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP Side Letter dated February 10, 2025, the Phase II Environmental Site Assessment by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC dated November 8, 2024, the RFP Response Narrative dated December 16, 2024, and the TIF District Summary Memorandum from Corporation Counsel dated March 5, 2025) and (ii) open items requiring further negotiation or factual confirmation. Bracketed notes are not operative contract text and are intended to be removed upon finalization. **]**

---

This DEVELOPMENT AGREEMENT (this "**Agreement**") is made and entered into as of [•], 2025 (the "**Effective Date**"), by and between the **CITY OF LAKEMONT, ILLINOIS**, an Illinois home rule municipality, approximate population 87,000, with offices at 200 Civic Center Drive, Lakemont, Illinois 60048 (the "**City**"), acting by and through its City Manager, Gerald T. Fong, pursuant to the authority granted by Resolution No. R-2025-017, and **GRANITE CITY DEVELOPMENT LLC**, an Illinois limited liability company formed on March 14, 2019, with its principal office located at 900 West Monroe Street, Suite 2200, Chicago, Illinois 60607 (the "**Developer**").

## RECITALS

**WHEREAS**, the City is the owner of certain real property commonly known as 1400–1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, Illinois 60048, consisting of three (3) parcels of record identified by Parcel Identification Numbers 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017, totaling approximately 12.7 acres (553,212 square feet) (collectively, the "**Property**"), being the former site of the Lakemont Ironworks manufacturing campus, which ceased industrial operations in 2009, and situated immediately adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra commuter rail station on the Metra Milwaukee District/North Line;

**WHEREAS**, the City, through its Community Development Department under the direction of Paul R. Vasquez, Director, conducted a competitive Request for Proposals process for the redevelopment of the Property as a mixed-use transit-oriented development, and selected Developer as the preferred developer based upon its qualifications, experience, project proposal, and demonstrated financial capacity;

> **[Drafting Note — RFP number conflict.** The Negotiated Term Sheet references "RFP No. 2024-CD-018," while the Developer's RFP Response Narrative references "RFP No. 2024-CD-07." Confirm the correct RFP number and conform all references. **]**

**WHEREAS**, Developer and the City executed a non-binding Negotiated Term Sheet dated January 22, 2025 (the "**Term Sheet**"), setting forth the principal economic and legal terms proposed to govern this Agreement;

**WHEREAS**, on February 18, 2025, the City Council of the City of Lakemont adopted Resolution No. R-2025-017 (the "**Resolution**"), granting conditional approval for the Project and authorizing the City Manager to negotiate and execute this Agreement, subject to the conditions set forth in Section 3 of the Resolution;

**WHEREAS**, the Property is currently zoned M-2 (General Manufacturing) and requires rezoning to PD-TOD (Planned Development–Transit Oriented District) to permit the proposed development program;

**WHEREAS**, the Property is located within the Lakemont Central Tax Increment Financing District, established by Ordinance No. 2019-42, expiring December 31, 2042 (the "**TIF District**");

**WHEREAS**, a Phase II Environmental Site Assessment was completed by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC in a report dated November 8, 2024 (the "**Phase II ESA**"), identifying certain environmental conditions on the Property requiring remediation;

**WHEREAS**, Developer has obtained a preliminary (non-binding) commitment from First Prairie National Bank (the "**Construction Lender**") for a construction loan facility in an amount of up to Two Hundred Ten Million Dollars ($210,000,000), subject to the execution of this Agreement in form and substance acceptable to the Construction Lender, and Developer's equity sources have committed a total of Eighty-Five Million Dollars ($85,000,000) in equity; and

**WHEREAS**, the City Council has determined that the Project will advance the public interest, health, safety, and welfare of the residents of the City by creating transit-oriented housing and commercial space, generating tax revenues, creating employment, providing affordable housing, remediating environmental contamination, and constructing public infrastructure and amenities.

**NOW, THEREFORE**, in consideration of the mutual covenants and agreements set forth herein, and other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the parties agree as follows:

---

## ARTICLE 1. DEFINITIONS

**1.1 Defined Terms.** In addition to terms defined elsewhere in this Agreement, the following capitalized terms shall have the meanings set forth below:

"**Additional Financing**" means the additional debt, mezzanine financing, and/or equity (estimated at approximately Fifty-Eight Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars ($58,400,000)) required to fund Phases III and IV, to be secured by Developer prior to commencement of each such phase.

"**Affordable Unit**" means a residential dwelling unit restricted to occupancy by households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of Area Median Income, as more particularly described in Section 8.1.

"**Agreement**" means this Development Agreement, including all Recitals, Articles, Sections, and Exhibits.

"**AMI**" means Area Median Income as published annually by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development for the Chicago-Naperville-Elgin, IL-IN-WI Metropolitan Statistical Area, or any successor designation.

"**Appraisal**" means the appraisal of the Property prepared by Crossland Appraisal Services, dated January 15, 2025, establishing an as-is fair market value of Eighteen Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars ($18,400,000).

"**Construction Lender**" means First Prairie National Bank, and any successor construction lender approved in accordance with Section 10.3.

"**Construction Loan Documents**" means the definitive loan agreements, mortgage, security instruments, guaranties, and other documentation evidencing the construction loan facility from the Construction Lender.

"**ELUC**" means an environmental land use control, in the form of a restrictive covenant, recorded against the Property in connection with the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program, as more particularly described in Section 6.2.

"**Force Majeure**" has the meaning set forth in Section 4.4.

"**IEPA**" means the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency.

"**NFR Letter**" means a No Further Remediation letter issued by the IEPA under the Voluntary Site Remediation Program.

"**Permitted Exceptions**" means the exceptions to title set forth in Exhibit [•], including: (i) real estate taxes not yet due and payable; (ii) standard utility and access easements not materially interfering with the Project; (iii) this Agreement; (iv) covenants and restrictions of the PD-TOD zoning ordinance; (v) environmental use controls or institutional controls recorded in connection with the IEPA VSRP NFR Letter and engineered barrier requirements, in each case as reviewed and approved by the Construction Lender's counsel; and (vi) such other exceptions as the Construction Lender may approve in writing.

"**Phase I**," "**Phase II**," "**Phase III**," and "**Phase IV**" mean the four phases of the Project as described in Section 3.1 and Exhibit [•] (Phasing Plan).

"**Project**" means the Lakemont Station Commons mixed-use transit-oriented development described in Article 3.

"**Property**" means the real property described in Section 2.1.

"**RAP**" means a Remedial Action Plan for the environmental remediation of Parcel 12-24-300-016, as more particularly described in Section 6.2.

"**Substantial Completion**" means, with respect to a phase of the Project, the completion of construction of such phase to the point where the building or improvements can be occupied and used for their intended purpose, as evidenced by the issuance of a temporary or final certificate of occupancy by the City's Building Department.

"**TIF Act**" means the Illinois Tax Increment Allocation Redevelopment Act, 65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-1 *et seq.*

"**TIF Reimbursement**" means the reimbursement of eligible redevelopment project costs from tax increment revenues generated within the TIF District, as more particularly described in Section 5.2.

"**VSRP**" means the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program.

**1.2 Incorporation of Recitals.** The Recitals set forth above are incorporated into and made a part of this Agreement as though fully set forth herein.

---

## ARTICLE 2. THE PROPERTY AND LAND CONVEYANCE

**2.1 The Property.** The Property consists of three (3) parcels of record located at 1400–1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, Illinois 60048, identified by Parcel Identification Numbers 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017, totaling approximately 12.7 acres (553,212 square feet), to be consolidated into a single parcel of record prior to or concurrent with conveyance to Developer, as more particularly described in the legal description attached as Exhibit [•].

> **[Drafting Note — Parcel acquisition history conflict.** The Resolution and the TIF District Summary Memorandum state that the City acquired Parcels 12-24-300-015 and 12-24-300-016 through tax forfeiture proceedings in 2016, and Parcel 12-24-300-017 by direct purchase in 2018. The Phase II ESA, however, states that Parcel 12-24-300-016 was acquired by the City through direct purchase in 2018 (together with Parcel 017), and that Parcel 12-24-300-015 was acquired through tax forfeiture in 2016. This discrepancy affects (i) the chain of title and encumbrances applicable to each parcel, (ii) the scope of the City's indemnification for pre-existing environmental conditions, and (iii) the City's responsible-party status under the Illinois Environmental Protection Act. Confirm the correct acquisition history for each parcel and conform the recitals and the City's representations in Section 15.2 accordingly. **]**

> **[Drafting Note — Parcel acreage conflict.** The Phase II ESA reports individual parcel sizes of approximately 4.2 acres (015), 4.1 acres (016), and 4.4 acres (017), while the RFP Response Narrative reports 3.8 acres (015), 4.1 acres (016), and 4.8 acres (017). The total of approximately 12.7 acres is consistent. Confirm the legal descriptions and exact acreages from the survey and consolidation plat. **]**

**2.2 Conveyance; Purchase Price.** Upon the satisfaction or waiver of all conditions precedent set forth in Article 16, the City shall convey the Property to Developer by special warranty deed, free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, restrictions, reversionary interests, rights of re-entry, possibilities of reverter, defeasible conditions, and other title defects or exceptions, except for the Permitted Exceptions. The purchase price for the Property shall be Nine Million Two Hundred Thousand Dollars ($9,200,000) (the "**Purchase Price**"), representing fifty percent (50%) of the appraised as-is fair market value of Eighteen Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars ($18,400,000) established in the Appraisal. The Purchase Price shall be due and payable at closing. Developer may fund the Purchase Price from equity, construction loan proceeds, or a combination thereof.

> **[Drafting Note — Reversionary interest prohibition (critical).** The Resolution, Condition No. 8, requires Developer to grant the City a reversionary interest in the Property if Phase I is not Substantially Complete within 36 months of conveyance. The Construction Lender's Term Sheet expressly prohibits any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, or defeasible fee estate, stating that incorporation of such an interest "would make the Loan impossible to close." Section 2.2 and Section 11 of this draft therefore provide for fee simple absolute title and substitute an alternative completion-assurance mechanism (see Section 11). This is the central open item and must be resolved with the City before the Construction Lender will commit. The Lakeshore Side Letter, Section 7(d), also requires Investor consent to any clawback mechanism. **]**

**2.3 Title Insurance.** The City shall deliver marketable and insurable fee simple title to the Property and shall provide, at the City's cost, a standard owner's title insurance policy in the amount of the Purchase Price issued by a nationally recognized title insurance company. Developer (and the Construction Lender) shall receive an ALTA lender's title insurance policy in the amount of the construction loan, with such endorsements as the Construction Lender may require, including zoning, comprehensive, survey, access, contiguity, planned development, environmental lien, and construction loan endorsements.

**2.4 Closing.** Closing of the conveyance of the Property (the "**Closing**") shall occur within thirty (30) days following the satisfaction or waiver of all conditions precedent set forth in Article 16. The parties' target Closing date is [mid-2025], subject to the timing of rezoning, IEPA approvals, and the satisfaction of other conditions.

**2.5 Phased Conveyance.** Notwithstanding the foregoing, the parties may agree to a phased conveyance of the Property, pursuant which Parcel 12-24-300-015 (which requires no remediation) and Parcel 12-24-300-017 (which requires only the engineered barrier) may be conveyed to Developer prior to completion of the remediation of Parcel 12-24-300-016, in order to permit early commencement of construction on the clean portions of the Property while remediation of Parcel 12-24-300-016 proceeds concurrently.

> **[Drafting Note — Phased conveyance vs. Resolution Condition No. 2 (open item).** Resolution Condition No. 2 requires that, "prior to any conveyance of the Property or any portion thereof to the Developer," the Developer shall have submitted and the IEPA shall have approved a RAP for Parcel 12-24-300-016. The Phase II ESA recommends a phased conveyance approach (conveying clean Parcels 015 and 017 first) to allow Phase I construction to begin on clean parcels while Parcel 016 remediation proceeds, noting that this could reduce project-wide delay risk. The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Section 7.1, condition 7) accepts, for Parcel 016, either (a) an NFR Letter or (b) VSRP enrollment with an approved RAP plus an environmental escrow/insurance product. The parties should confirm whether the Resolution's "prior to any conveyance" language requires RAP approval before conveyance of the clean parcels, or only before conveyance of Parcel 016, and whether a phased conveyance is compatible with the PD-TOD rezoning and parcel consolidation requirements. Developer's preference is phased conveyance to protect the Phase I groundbreaking deadline. **]**

---

## ARTICLE 3. THE PROJECT AND DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM

**3.1 The Project.** Developer shall design, entitle, construct, and deliver a mixed-use transit-oriented development known as "Lakemont Station Commons" (the "**Project**"), with a total gross building area of approximately 1,620,000 square feet across four phases, comprising the following program elements:

| Component | Phase I | Phase II | Phase III | Phase IV | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Residential Units | 320 (256 market + 64 affordable) | — | 210 (168 market + 42 affordable) | — | 530 |
| Affordable Units (≤60% AMI) | 64 | — | 42 | — | 106 (20%) |
| Office (sq ft) | — | 280,000 | — | — | 280,000 |
| Retail (sq ft) | 45,000 | 18,000 | — | — | 63,000 |
| Hotel (rooms) | — | — | 150 | — | 150 |
| Community Center (sq ft) | — | — | — | 28,000 | 28,000 |
| Public Plaza (acres) | — | — | — | 2.5 | 2.5 |
| Parking (spaces) | 480 | 350 | — | 600 | 1,430 |
| Gross Building Area (sq ft) | 420,000 | 380,000 | 440,000 | 380,000 | 1,620,000 |
| Estimated Hard Construction Cost | $105,000,000 | $76,000,000 | $88,000,000 | $71,000,000 | $340,000,000 |

The total estimated Project cost, inclusive of soft costs, land, financing, environmental remediation, and infrastructure, is approximately Four Hundred Twelve Million Dollars ($412,000,000). The detailed development program, including site plan, phasing plan, and project budget, is set forth in Exhibit [•].

> **[Drafting Note — Affordable unit count conflict.** The Resolution (Condition No. 7) and the RFP Response Narrative require not less than twenty percent (20%) of total residential units to be affordable, which equals 106 units. The Term Sheet's summary table (Section 3.5) references "at least 100 affordable units," while its detailed breakdown (Section 12) confirms 106 units. The 20% / 106-unit figure is the binding requirement. This draft uses 106 units. Confirm that the City accepts 106 as the fixed obligation (not a higher number) and that the "at least 100" language in the Term Sheet summary is superseded. **]**

**3.2 Phase Descriptions.** The four phases of the Project shall be as follows:

**(a) Phase I — Residential Tower A and Retail Podium.** Three hundred twenty (320) residential units (256 market-rate and 64 affordable), 45,000 square feet of ground-floor retail, and a 480-space structured parking facility; approximately 420,000 square feet of gross building area; estimated hard construction cost of $105,000,000.

**(b) Phase II — Office Building and Retail.** Two hundred eighty thousand (280,000) square feet of Class A office space, 18,000 square feet of retail, and a 350-space structured parking facility; approximately 380,000 square feet of gross building area; estimated hard construction cost of $76,000,000.

**(c) Phase III — Residential Tower B and Hotel.** Two hundred ten (210) residential units (168 market-rate and 42 affordable) and a 150-room select-service hotel; approximately 440,000 square feet of gross building area; estimated hard construction cost of $88,000,000.

**(d) Phase IV — Public Plaza, Community Center, and Parking Structure.** A 2.5-acre public plaza, a 28,000-square-foot community center (to be conveyed to the City upon completion), and a 600-space public parking structure; approximately 380,000 square feet of gross building area; estimated hard construction cost of $71,000,000.

**3.3 Community Center Conveyance.** Upon Substantial Completion of Phase IV, Developer shall convey the community center to the City in fee simple, by special warranty deed, at no additional cost to the City. The community center shall be designed in consultation with the City's Parks and Recreation Department. The program, design specifications, and scope of furniture, fixtures, and equipment to be included in the conveyance shall be set forth in Exhibit [•].

> **[Drafting Note — Community center FF&E (open item).** The Term Sheet states the community center is to be conveyed "at no additional cost" to the City, while the RFP Response Narrative states it will be conveyed "fully furnished and equipped per the agreed-upon specifications." The scope and cost of furniture, fixtures, and equipment (FF&E) to be included in the conveyance, and whether such FF&E is TIF-eligible, must be defined and capped in Exhibit [•]. Developer's position: the conveyance obligation should be limited to the building shell and core systems plus a defined, capped FF&E package, with any FF&E beyond the defined scope at the City's expense. **]**

**3.4 Design Review.** Developer shall submit plans and specifications for each phase to the City's Community Development Department for design review and approval. The City shall review and respond to submissions within [thirty (30)] business days. The City's approval shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed. The City's review shall be limited to conformity with the PD-TOD zoning standards, this Agreement, and applicable codes, and shall not extend to aesthetic matters within Developer's reasonable design discretion except as expressly set forth in the PD-TOD ordinance.

---

## ARTICLE 4. PHASING AND CONSTRUCTION SCHEDULE

**4.1 Phasing Schedule.** Developer shall construct the Project in four phases in accordance with the following schedule:

| Phase | Commencement Deadline | Substantial Completion Deadline |
|---|---|---|
| Phase I | Groundbreaking no later than March 1, 2026 | September 30, 2028 |
| Phase II | Within 12 months of Phase I Substantial Completion | Within 24 months of Phase II commencement |
| Phase III | Within 12 months of Phase II Substantial Completion | Within 30 months of Phase III commencement |
| Phase IV | Concurrent with Phase III commencement | Within 24 months of Phase IV commencement |

**4.2 Phase I Commencement.** Phase I groundbreaking shall occur no later than March 1, 2026, provided that the City has (a) completed the rezoning of the Property to PD-TOD, (b) completed or authorized parcel consolidation, (c) conveyed the Property (or the applicable clean parcels, in the case of a phased conveyance) to Developer, and (d) commenced or completed the off-site infrastructure improvements necessary to support Phase I, in each case in accordance with the timelines set forth in this Agreement.

> **[Drafting Note — Phase I commencement window conflict (critical).** The Term Sheet (Section 15) permits the City to terminate the Agreement if Developer fails to commence Phase I construction within six (6) months following land conveyance. If Closing occurs in mid-2025 (e.g., June 2025), the six-month window would expire in December 2025 — approximately three months before the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking deadline. This draft deletes the six-month commencement trigger and ties commencement solely to the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking deadline, which is the operative milestone in both the Term Sheet and the Resolution. Confirm the City's agreement to remove the six-month trigger or to measure it from a date that does not precede the March 1, 2026 deadline. **]**

**4.3 Construction Progress Reports.** Developer shall provide quarterly construction progress reports to the City's Community Development Department, including percentage of completion, updated schedule, and identification of any delays or anticipated deviations, within thirty (30) days following the end of each calendar quarter during which active construction is occurring.

**4.4 Force Majeure.**

**(a) Definition.** "Force Majeure" means acts of God, fire, flood, earthquake, hurricane, tornado, severe weather events, pandemic, epidemic, quarantine, governmental health orders, war, terrorism, civil unrest, insurrection, strikes or labor disputes (other than those caused by Developer's labor practices or failure to comply with applicable labor laws), industry-wide supply chain disruptions beyond Developer's reasonable control, governmental moratoria on construction, and actions or inactions of governmental authorities (other than the City) that prevent or materially delay construction. Force Majeure shall not include financial inability to perform, changes in market conditions, increases in the cost of labor or materials (unless attributable to an industry-wide supply chain disruption), Developer's failure to obtain financing, or any event caused by Developer's negligence or willful misconduct.

**(b) Effect.** Force Majeure events shall extend the applicable deadlines for commencement and Substantial Completion on a day-for-day basis for the duration of the Force Majeure event.

**(c) Aggregate Cap.** The maximum extension of time available under this Section 4.4 shall not exceed eighteen (18) months in the aggregate across the Project.

**(d) Notice and Mitigation.** Developer shall provide written notice to the City within fifteen (15) business days of the occurrence of a Force Majeure event and shall use commercially reasonable efforts to mitigate its effects.

> **[Drafting Note — Force majeure cap conflict.** The Term Sheet provides an 18-month aggregate force majeure cap across the Project. The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Section 9, condition 9) permits force majeure extensions of only up to 180 days (six months) for the Phase I Substantial Completion default. The Lender's shorter 180-day limit could cause a loan default even where the City has granted a longer extension under this Agreement. Developer should negotiate with the Construction Lender to align the force majeure periods, or structure the Lender's default to track the City-approved extensions. **]**

> **[Drafting Note — Remediation delay as force majeure (open item).** The Phase II ESA recommends that the transactional documents address whether delay in issuance of the NFR Letter for Parcel 12-24-300-016 (estimated 14–18 months, but potentially longer) constitutes a Force Majeure event, a basis for deadline extension, or a potential default. Given that the contamination is pre-existing and the remediation timeline is subject to IEPA review beyond Developer's control, Developer's position is that regulatory delays in NFR Letter issuance should constitute Force Majeure or an automatic deadline extension. Confirm. **]**

---

## ARTICLE 5. PUBLIC INCENTIVES

**5.1 Land Write-Down.** The conveyance of the Property at a Purchase Price of $9,200,000, representing 50% of the appraised fair market value, constitutes a land write-down incentive with a value of $9,200,000. The land write-down shall constitute a separate incentive and shall not be charged against, or reduce, the TIF Reimbursement cap set forth in Section 5.2.

> **[Drafting Note — Land write-down vs. TIF cap (open item).** The TIF District Summary Memorandum (Section VII) flags that, if the City records the land write-down as a TIF expenditure from the Special Tax Allocation Fund, it counts against the $48,000,000 TIF cap, and recommends the development agreement specify whether the write-down is charged against the cap or funded from another source. Developer's position, reflected in Section 5.1, is that the land write-down is a separate incentive funded from the City's general economic development resources and does not reduce the TIF cap. Confirm the City's agreement and the funding source. **]**

**5.2 Tax Increment Financing.**

**(a) TIF Reimbursement Commitment.** The City shall pledge up to Forty-Eight Million Dollars ($48,000,000) in tax increment revenues over the remaining life of the TIF District (expiring December 31, 2042) to reimburse Developer for eligible redevelopment project costs incurred in connection with the Project, including without limitation public infrastructure improvements, environmental remediation costs, parking structure construction costs, and other eligible project costs under the TIF Act and the Lakemont Central TIF District Redevelopment Plan adopted by Ordinance No. 2019-42.

**(b) Pay-As-You-Go.** TIF Reimbursement shall be on a pay-as-you-go basis. Developer shall front all eligible costs and shall be reimbursed annually from available tax increment as generated by the Project and other developments within the TIF District. The City shall not issue TIF bonds or notes for the Project.

**(c) Reimbursement Priority.** TIF Reimbursement shall be made in the following priority order: (i) first, environmental remediation costs for Parcels 12-24-300-016 and 12-24-300-017; (ii) second, Developer's eligible on-site infrastructure expenditures; (iii) third, public parking structure, public plaza, and community center costs associated with Phase IV; and (iv) fourth, all other eligible costs, including professional services and financing costs.

**(d) City Efforts to Maximize Reimbursement.** The City covenants to use good-faith efforts to maximize the tax increment available for TIF Reimbursement, including by seeking an extension of the TIF District or the establishment of a successor TIF district if, as of a benchmark date of December 31, 2038, cumulative TIF Reimbursement is tracking materially below the projections set forth in Exhibit [•].

**(e) No Reduction for Abatement.** The Phase I property tax abatement set forth in Section 5.4 shall not be structured or applied in a manner that reduces the tax increment available for TIF Reimbursement below the projections set forth in Exhibit [•] to the extent legally permissible.

> **[Drafting Note — TIF shortfall risk (critical open item).** The TIF District Summary Memorandum (Section IV) concludes that, under a base-case projection without abatement impact, cumulative increment reaches approximately $49.3 million by 2041 and $54.2 million by 2042 (exceeding the $48 million cap). However, under an adjusted scenario incorporating the Phase I property tax abatement, cumulative increment through 2042 is estimated at only approximately $38 million to $42 million — potentially $6 million to $10 million short of the $48 million cap. Corporation Counsel recommends that the development agreement (i) make TIF Reimbursement expressly contingent on actual increment collected and not a guaranteed City obligation, (ii) provide that a TIF shortfall does not constitute a City default, and (iii) include a City covenant to use good-faith efforts to seek a TIF extension. From the Developer's perspective, the Developer fronts all eligible costs and bears the timing and shortfall risk under a pay-as-you-go structure. Developer's positions reflected in this draft: (a) the reimbursement priority in Section 5.2(c) ensures Developer's most critical fronted costs (remediation and on-site infrastructure) are reimbursed first; (b) the City's good-faith extension covenant in Section 5.2(d); and (c) the abatement-no-reduction provision in Section 5.2(e). The parties should further negotiate whether the City will provide any backstop or alternative funding mechanism for a material TIF shortfall, and whether the Developer's obligation to construct later phases should be conditioned on TIF performance. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 4.3(a)) requires Investor consent to any change to the TIF reimbursement cap or eligible cost categories. **]**

> **[Drafting Note — Abatement-TIF interaction (open item).** Corporation Counsel notes an internal tension: the Phase I abatement benefits Developer by reducing its property tax burden but simultaneously reduces the TIF increment available for reimbursement. Corporation Counsel suggests structuring the abatement to apply only to the non-TIF-captured portion of the tax bill, if legally permissible, or obtaining an express acknowledgment that the abatement reduces TIF capacity. Section 5.2(e) adopts the former approach. Confirm legal feasibility with the City and outside counsel. **]**

**5.3 Infrastructure Cost-Sharing.**

**(a) City Off-Site Infrastructure.** The City shall fund Twelve Million Five Hundred Thousand Dollars ($12,500,000) for off-site infrastructure improvements, including the widening of Lakemont Station Boulevard, installation of a new traffic signal at the Oak Street intersection, extension of water main service, and upgrade of sanitary sewer capacity. The City's off-site infrastructure funding shall be appropriated from the City's general revenue or capital improvement funds and shall not be charged against, or reduce, the TIF Reimbursement cap.

**(b) Developer On-Site Infrastructure.** Developer shall fund Eight Million Three Hundred Thousand Dollars ($8,300,000) for on-site infrastructure improvements, including internal roadways, utilities, stormwater management, pedestrian pathways, and landscape improvements. Developer's eligible on-site infrastructure expenditures shall be reimbursable from TIF Reimbursement subject to the priority in Section 5.2(c) and Developer's substantiation of the public character of each component.

**(c) Total Infrastructure Budget.** The total combined infrastructure budget is Twenty Million Eight Hundred Thousand Dollars ($20,800,000).

**(d) City Infrastructure Timing.** The City's off-site infrastructure improvements shall be completed on a schedule coordinated with the Phase I construction timeline, and in any event the City shall complete the improvements necessary to support Phase I occupancy no later than [the date that is six (6) months prior to Phase I Substantial Completion]. The City's failure to fund or complete its committed infrastructure obligations within the timelines specified herein shall constitute a material default by the City and a basis for Developer termination under Section 12.3.

> **[Drafting Note — City infrastructure funding source (open item).** The TIF District Summary Memorandum (Section VII) flags that, if the City's $12.5 million infrastructure commitment is funded from TIF, it counts against the $48 million cap; if funded from general revenue or capital improvement funds, it does not. Section 5.3(a) provides for funding from general revenue/capital funds. Confirm the City's funding source. **]**

**5.4 Property Tax Abatement.** The City shall provide a twelve (12)-year declining property tax abatement on Phase I improvements only, as follows: Years 1–4, 75% abatement; Years 5–8, 50% abatement; Years 9–12, 25% abatement; Year 13 and thereafter, 0% (full assessment). The abatement shall apply to the incremental increase in assessed value attributable to the Phase I improvements, measured from the pre-development base equalized assessed value. No property tax abatement is provided for Phase II, Phase III, or Phase IV.

> **[Drafting Note — Abatement for later phases (open item).** The Term Sheet (Section 18.4) confirms that no abatement is provided for Phases II, III, or IV, and states that the parties' respective positions regarding the financial structure of later phases, including any additional incentives, shall be addressed during negotiation of the definitive Agreement. Developer may seek additional incentives for later phases; this is an open item. **]**

**5.5 Building Permit Fee Waiver.** The City shall waive building permit fees for Phase I and Phase II of the Project, with an estimated combined value of One Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars ($1,400,000) ($920,000 for Phase I and $480,000 for Phase II). No permit fee waiver is contemplated for Phase III or Phase IV. The permit fee waivers shall constitute a separate incentive and shall not be charged against the TIF Reimbursement cap.

**5.6 Summary of Public Incentives.** The public incentives are summarized as follows:

| Incentive | Value |
|---|---|
| Land Write-Down | $9,200,000 |
| TIF Reimbursement (up to) | $48,000,000 |
| City Off-Site Infrastructure Funding | $12,500,000 |
| Property Tax Abatement (Phase I, 12 years) | To be calculated based on actual assessed values |
| Permit Fee Waivers (Phases I & II) | $1,400,000 |

## ARTICLE 6. ENVIRONMENTAL MATTERS

**6.1 Environmental Conditions.** The Phase II ESA identified the following environmental conditions on the Property:

**(a) Parcel 12-24-300-015.** No environmental contamination requiring further action; no further investigation or remediation required.

**(b) Parcel 12-24-300-016.** Petroleum hydrocarbon contamination (BTEX compounds — benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene) in soil and groundwater over approximately 1.8 acres, plus localized trichloroethylene (TCE) contamination. Remediation under the IEPA VSRP is required, at an estimated cost of $2,800,000 and an estimated timeline of fourteen (14) to eighteen (18) months, culminating in issuance of an NFR Letter.

**(c) Parcel 12-24-300-017.** Low-level metals contamination (primarily arsenic) in shallow fill material over approximately 3.2 acres, suitable for residential use with installation of an engineered barrier (clean fill cap) and recording of an ELUC, at an estimated cost of $340,000.

The total estimated environmental remediation cost is Three Million One Hundred Forty Thousand Dollars ($3,140,000).

**6.2 Remediation Responsibilities.**

**(a) VSRP Enrollment and RAP.** Developer shall enroll Parcel 12-24-300-016 in the IEPA VSRP and shall prepare and submit a RAP for IEPA review and approval. The RAP shall address both the BTEX and TCE contamination and shall provide for issuance of an NFR Letter upon completion. Developer shall engage Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC (or a successor environmental consultant reasonably acceptable to the City) to perform the remediation.

**(b) Engineered Barrier.** Developer shall install the engineered barrier (clean fill cap) on Parcel 12-24-300-017 and shall record the ELUC against the Property.

**(c) NFR Letter.** Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to obtain the NFR Letter for Parcel 12-24-300-016 prior to commencement of vertical construction on the contaminated portion of that parcel. Construction on Parcels 12-24-300-015 and 12-24-300-017 may proceed independently of the Parcel 12-24-300-016 remediation schedule.

**(d) Vapor Intrusion.** Developer shall conduct a vapor intrusion screening evaluation for Parcel 12-24-300-016 in accordance with IEPA guidance prior to finalizing building designs for structures on or near the contaminated area, and shall incorporate vapor mitigation measures (such as sub-slab depressurization systems, vapor barriers, or passive venting) into building designs as warranted.

> **[Drafting Note — Remediation performer / VSRP applicant (open item).** The Phase II ESA (Section 8.2(a)) notes that the City, as the current property owner at the time of contamination awareness, bears primary responsible-party status under the Illinois Environmental Protection Act, but that VSRP enrollment by a prospective purchaser is common in brownfield transactions. Resolution Condition No. 2 requires Developer to submit the RAP, implying Developer is the VSRP applicant. Section 6.2 provides for Developer to perform the remediation (consistent with the Resolution), with the City funding and indemnifying as set forth in Sections 6.3 and 6.4. The parties should confirm: (i) which party is the named VSRP applicant; (ii) which party engages and supervises the environmental consultant; and (iii) which party is responsible for regulatory interaction with IEPA. Developer's preference is to control the remediation schedule (to protect the Phase I timeline) while the City funds the cost and indemnifies for pre-existing conditions. **]**

**6.3 Remediation Cost Allocation.**

**(a) Base Remediation Cost.** The City shall be responsible for the base environmental remediation costs of Three Million One Hundred Forty Thousand Dollars ($3,140,000), which shall be reimbursed to Developer (or paid directly to the environmental consultant) from TIF Reimbursement as a first-priority eligible cost pursuant to Section 5.2(c), or, at Developer's election, paid by the City directly.

**(b) Cost Overruns.** The City shall be responsible for environmental remediation costs that exceed the base estimate of $3,140,000, including costs arising from discovery of additional contamination beyond the currently delineated plume extent, extended groundwater treatment, supplemental IEPA requirements, or vapor mitigation measures attributable to pre-existing contamination. The Phase II ESA recommends contingency of 25% to 35% above the base estimate, with total cost exposure potentially reaching $3.8 million to $4.2 million for Parcel 12-24-300-016 alone.

> **[Drafting Note — Cost overrun responsibility (critical open item).** The Phase II ESA (Section 4.2.4 and Section 8.2(c)) expressly recommends that the transactional documents establish a clear mechanism for allocating costs exceeding the $3,140,000 base estimate, noting that actual costs could exceed the base by 25% to 35%. The Term Sheet provides that the City shall indemnify Developer for pre-existing environmental conditions but does not clearly allocate cost overruns. Section 6.3(b) places overrun responsibility on the City, consistent with the pre-existing nature of the contamination and the City's indemnification. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 4.3(d)) requires Investor consent for any acceptance of environmental remediation obligations exceeding $3,140,000 without a corresponding increase in TIF reimbursement or direct City funding. Confirm the City's agreement to bear overruns, and the mechanism (direct City payment, increased TIF reimbursement, or both). **]**

> **[Drafting Note — Vapor intrusion costs (open item).** The Phase II ESA recommends a vapor intrusion evaluation and possible vapor mitigation systems for structures on or near the Parcel 12-24-300-016 contaminated area. These costs are not included in the $3,140,000 estimate. Section 6.2(d) requires the evaluation, and Section 6.3(b) treats vapor mitigation attributable to pre-existing contamination as a City responsibility. Confirm. **]**

**6.4 City Environmental Indemnification.** The City shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Developer, its members, officers, employees, agents, contractors, and successors from and against all claims, demands, actions, losses, costs, damages, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to pre-existing environmental conditions on the Property (Parcels 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017), including without limitation third-party claims, governmental enforcement actions, and contribution or cost-recovery actions under federal or state environmental laws. This indemnification shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement.

> **[Drafting Note — Scope of City indemnification (open item).** The Term Sheet (Section 16) and this Section 6.4 provide for City indemnification of pre-existing environmental conditions. The Phase II ESA confirms that the contamination on Parcels 016 and 017 is pre-existing and attributable to the former Lakemont Ironworks operations (ceased 2009) and historical fill placement (1970s–1990s), all of which predate Developer's involvement. The City acquired the parcels with knowledge of the environmental conditions (per the ESA, the City's 2018 purchase records reference a prior Phase I ESA identifying the UST farm and metal finishing operations). Developer's position is that the City's indemnification should be broad and survive conveyance. Confirm the City's agreement to a broad, surviving indemnity, and address whether the indemnity is capped or uncapped. **]**

**6.5 Environmental Insurance.** Developer shall obtain pollution legal liability insurance in an amount not less than Ten Million Dollars ($10,000,000), naming the City and the Construction Lender as additional insured, with a policy term covering the full construction period and a three-year extended reporting period.

**6.6 Reliance Letters.** Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to cause Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC to execute reliance letters permitting the City, the Construction Lender, and Developer's equity investors to rely on the Phase II ESA, on terms reasonably acceptable to all parties.

> **[Drafting Note — ESA reliance (open item).** The Phase II ESA (Confidentiality Notice and Section 9) states that the report was prepared for the exclusive use of Developer and its counsel (Thornfield & Pratt LLP), and that reliance by third parties (including the City, the Construction Lender, and Lakeshore Capital) requires execution of a reliance letter from GEC. The Construction Lender requires environmental clearance as a condition to closing. Confirm that reliance letters will be obtained and allocate the cost thereof. **]**

**6.7 Institutional Controls as Permitted Exceptions.** The ELUCs and institutional controls recorded in connection with the IEPA VSRP NFR Letter (Parcel 12-24-300-016) and the engineered barrier (Parcel 12-24-300-017) shall constitute Permitted Exceptions, provided they are reviewed and approved by the Construction Lender's counsel and do not materially impair Developer's fee simple title or the Construction Lender's security interest.

> **[Drafting Note — ELUC encumbrances (open item).** The Phase II ESA (Section 4.3.3 and Section 6.2) notes that the ELUC to be recorded against Parcel 12-24-300-017 (and potentially a groundwater use restriction ELUC for Parcel 12-24-300-016) constitutes an encumbrance on title. The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Section 4.2) permits such institutional controls as Permitted Exceptions only if reviewed and approved by the Lender's counsel. The consolidation plat should expressly reference the institutional control area by legal description. Confirm Lender approval of the ELUC form. **]**

## ARTICLE 7. PERFORMANCE SECURITY

**7.1 Performance Bond / Letter of Credit.** Prior to the conveyance of the Property to Developer, Developer shall post a performance bond in the amount of Fifteen Million Dollars ($15,000,000), issued by Ridgepoint Surety Corporation (or another surety company reasonably acceptable to the City), or an irrevocable standby letter of credit in the equivalent amount issued by a nationally recognized financial institution acceptable to the City (collectively, the "**Performance Security**"), securing Developer's obligation to commence and substantially complete Phase I in accordance with this Agreement.

**7.2 Maintenance and Release.** The Performance Security shall be maintained continuously from the date of conveyance until Phase I Substantial Completion, as evidenced by issuance of a temporary or final certificate of occupancy. Upon Phase I Substantial Completion, the Performance Security shall be released and returned to Developer within thirty (30) days following written request and verification of Substantial Completion by the City.

**7.3 Performance Security for Later Phases.** The amount, form, and conditions of any performance security for Phases II, III, and IV shall be established in this Agreement or by amendment, and shall be reasonable in relation to the scope and cost of each phase.

> **[Drafting Note — Performance security dual use (open item).** The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Section 4.1) requires assignment of the $15 million performance bond/letter of credit as collateral for the construction loan. Thus the Performance Security serves dual purposes: City completion security and Construction Lender collateral. The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Section 12) also suggests an additional escrow deposit or letter of credit as an alternative clawback mechanism (see Section 11). The parties should coordinate to avoid requiring duplicative security that would unreasonably increase Developer's capital requirements. Developer's position: the existing $15 million Performance Security should satisfy both the City's and the Lender's requirements to the extent practicable, and any additional clawback security should be limited. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 4.3(e)) requires Investor consent for any modification to performance bond/letter of credit requirements. **]**
