## ARTICLE 8. COMMUNITY BENEFITS

**8.1 Affordable Housing.**

**(a) Minimum Affordability.** Not less than twenty percent (20%) of the total residential dwelling units constructed in the Project (106 units) shall be designated as Affordable Units available to households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of AMI. The Affordable Units shall be distributed as follows: 64 Affordable Units in Phase I (out of 320 total) and 42 Affordable Units in Phase III (out of 210 total).

**(b) Integration.** Affordable Units shall be distributed proportionally across unit types (studio, one-bedroom, and two-bedroom) and shall not be segregated or concentrated on particular floors or in separate wings. Affordable Units shall be comparable in design, finishes, and amenity access to market-rate units within the same building, subject to reasonable variations consistent with industry practice.

**(c) Affordability Period.** Affordability restrictions shall remain in effect for a minimum of thirty (30) years from the date of issuance of the certificate of occupancy for the applicable phase, memorialized by a restrictive covenant recorded against the applicable parcel and running with the land, in a form to be set forth in Exhibit [•].

**(d) Compliance.** Developer shall comply with the City's Affordable Housing Compliance Program and shall provide annual income certification documentation to the City's Community Development Department for all Affordable Units throughout the affordability restriction period.

**8.2 Local Hiring.**

**(a) Goal.** Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to achieve a goal that not less than thirty-five percent (35%) of all construction labor hours on the Project be performed by individuals who are residents of the City of Lakemont. This goal shall be measured on a cumulative project-wide basis.

**(b) Good-Faith Compliance.** Failure to achieve the 35% local hiring goal in any given quarter or on a cumulative basis shall not, in and of itself, constitute a default under this Agreement, provided that Developer demonstrates good-faith efforts to achieve the goal, including posting job openings with the City's workforce development partners, attending local job fairs, and providing reasonable notice of hiring opportunities to the Community Development Department.

**(c) Reporting.** Developer shall submit quarterly reports to the City's Community Development Department documenting local hiring percentages, including workforce data by trade category, residency information, and total labor hours, within thirty (30) days following the end of each calendar quarter during which active construction is occurring.

> **[Drafting Note — Local hiring: goal vs. requirement (open item).** Resolution Condition No. 3(c)(i) refers to "a local hiring goal requiring that not less than thirty-five percent (35%)" of construction labor hours, which is ambiguous as to whether 35% is a hard requirement or a good-faith goal. The Term Sheet (Section 9) treats it as a good-faith goal, with failure to achieve not constituting a default per se. Section 8.2 adopts the Term Sheet's good-faith-goal approach, which is favorable to Developer. Confirm the City's agreement that 35% is a good-faith goal, not a hard requirement or default trigger. **]**

**8.3 Living Wage.** Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to require that all permanent employees employed at retail establishments and hotel operations within the Project be compensated at a wage rate of not less than Seventeen Dollars and Fifty Cents ($17.50) per hour, through lease provisions and hotel operating agreements; provided, however, that Developer's obligation shall be limited to including such requirement in its standard lease and operating forms, and Developer shall not be liable for the wage practices of independent commercial tenants or hotel operators except as expressly required by their leases or operating agreements.

> **[Drafting Note — Living wage / first-source hiring gap (open item).** Resolution Condition No. 3(c) requires (ii) a living wage of not less than $17.50/hour for all permanent employees at retail and hotel operations, and (iii) a first-source hiring program partnering with the Lakemont Workforce Center. The Term Sheet (Section 9) is silent on both the living wage and the first-source hiring program. Section 8.3 incorporates the living wage as a commercially reasonable effort through lease/operating provisions (limiting Developer's direct liability for tenant/tenant wage practices), and Section 8.4 incorporates the first-source hiring program. The scope of Developer's obligation — particularly whether the living wage applies to all permanent employees or only those directly employed by Developer — and the consequences of tenant non-compliance require negotiation. Developer's position: the living wage obligation should be a tenant/lease-level obligation, not a direct Developer obligation, and non-compliance by a tenant should not constitute a Developer default absent a failure to include the requirement in the lease. **]**

**8.4 First-Source Hiring.** Developer and its commercial tenants shall partner with the Lakemont Workforce Center for the recruitment, training, and hiring of individuals to fill permanent employment positions created within the Project. Developer shall notify the Lakemont Workforce Center of available permanent positions prior to broader recruitment, giving Lakemont residents priority access to employment opportunities.

**8.5 Community Benefits Agreement.** The community benefits provisions set forth in this Article 8 shall be deemed to satisfy the Community Benefits Agreement or Community Benefits provisions required by Resolution Condition No. 3(c), and no separate Community Benefits Agreement with third-party community organizations shall be required unless mutually agreed by the parties.

> **[Drafting Note — CBA form (open item).** Resolution Condition No. 3(c) requires the Development Agreement to "incorporate a Community Benefits Agreement (CBA) or Community Benefits provisions." Section 8.5 treats the provisions of Article 8 as satisfying this requirement. Confirm whether the City requires a separate CBA with community organizations, or whether the provisions of Article 8 are sufficient. **]**

## ARTICLE 9. CONSTRUCTION STANDARDS

**9.1 Prevailing Wage.** All construction work performed on the Project that constitutes "public works" within the meaning of the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act (820 ILCS 130/0.01 *et seq.*) (the "**PWA**") shall be subject to the prevailing wage requirements of the PWA. Developer shall include prevailing wage requirements in all contracts and subcontracts for construction work that constitutes public works, shall maintain certified payroll records and make them available to the City and the Illinois Department of Labor upon request, and shall post prevailing wage rate schedules at the construction site.

> **[Drafting Note — Prevailing wage scope conflict (critical open item).** The Term Sheet (Section 11.1) states that "all construction performed on the Project shall comply with the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act," while the Resolution (Section 8) limits prevailing wage to construction work "that constitutes 'public works' within the meaning of the" PWA. The TIF District Summary Memorandum (Section V) concludes that, given the substantial public financial assistance (land write-down, TIF, abatement, fee waivers), the PWA "likely applies to all construction activity on the project site," and recommends the development agreement specify the scope with precision, noting that the Developer's counsel will likely resist coverage for purely private components. Section 9.1 adopts the narrower "public works" formulation from the Resolution, which is favorable to Developer and limits prevailing wage cost exposure on private components (market-rate residential, office, hotel). Corporation Counsel's position is that the broad interpretation (all construction) is the City's strongest position. This is a significant cost issue and a key negotiation point. The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Section 6.1) requires compliance with the PWA "to the extent applicable," consistent with the narrower formulation. **]**

**9.2 LEED Certification.** All buildings within the Project shall be designed and constructed to achieve LEED Gold certification (or an equivalent green building certification) as administered by the U.S. Green Building Council. Developer shall provide evidence of LEED registration for each building prior to commencement of construction and shall submit documentation of certification status upon receipt. The specific LEED rating system applicable to each building component shall be identified in Exhibit [•].

> **[Drafting Note — "Equivalent" determination (open item).** Resolution Condition No. 6 and the Term Sheet (Section 11.2) permit "an equivalent green building certification as determined acceptable by the Director of the Community Development Department." Developer's position: the standard for "equivalent" should be objective (e.g., a nationally recognized green building standard with comparable point thresholds) rather than subject to the Director's unfettered discretion, to provide certainty. Confirm. **]**

**9.3 Insurance.** Developer shall maintain, or cause its general contractor to maintain, throughout the construction period for each phase, the following minimum insurance coverages:

| Coverage | Minimum Amount |
|---|---|
| Commercial General Liability (CGL) | $10,000,000 per occurrence / $25,000,000 aggregate |
| Umbrella/Excess Liability | $25,000,000 per occurrence and aggregate |
| Builder's Risk | Full replacement value of each phase under construction (all-risk basis) |
| Workers' Compensation | Statutory limits |
| Automobile Liability | $5,000,000 combined single limit |
| Professional Liability (Architect/Engineer) | $5,000,000 |
| Pollution Legal Liability | $10,000,000 |

The City and the Construction Lender shall be named as additional insured and/or loss payee, as applicable. All carriers shall be rated A- VII or better by A.M. Best. All policies shall provide not less than thirty (30) days' prior written notice of cancellation or material modification.

> **[Drafting Note — Insurance requirements conflict.** The Term Sheet (Section 11.3) specifies CGL aggregate of $20,000,000 and does not include Professional Liability or Pollution Legal Liability. The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Section 5) specifies CGL aggregate of $25,000,000 and adds Professional Liability ($5,000,000) and Pollution Legal Liability ($10,000,000). Section 9.3 adopts the Construction Lender's higher/more comprehensive requirements to satisfy the Lender. Confirm the City's agreement to the higher CGL aggregate and the additional coverages, and allocate the incremental cost. **]**

## ARTICLE 10. ASSIGNMENT AND CHANGE OF CONTROL

**10.1 Restriction on Assignment.** Developer may not assign this Agreement or any interest therein, in whole or in part, without the prior written consent of the City, which consent may be withheld in the City's reasonable discretion; provided, however, that the City's consent shall not be required for any Permitted Transfer (as defined below).

**10.2 Permitted Transfers.** The following transfers shall not require the City's prior written consent and shall not constitute a default under this Agreement:

**(a)** Assignment to an entity in which Marcus J. Pellegrini (directly or through the Pellegrini Family Trust) holds a controlling interest and which entity expressly assumes in writing all of Developer's obligations under this Agreement;

**(b)** Transfers of membership interests among the existing members of Developer (the Pellegrini Family Trust, Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, and TransitWorks Equity Group LLC) that do not result in Marcus J. Pellegrini ceasing to serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer of Developer;

**(c)** Transfers by Marcus J. Pellegrini for estate planning purposes to family trusts or entities controlled by Pellegrini, provided Pellegrini retains management control;

**(d)** Transfers of non-managing limited partnership interests in Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP among its existing or admitted limited partners; and

**(e)** Capital raises or admissions of new members approved by both the City (if required under Section 10.1) and Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP (as required under the Lakeshore Side Letter), provided that Marcus J. Pellegrini continues to serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer of Developer and to exercise day-to-day management authority over the Project.

Developer shall provide the City with written notice of any Permitted Transfer, together with a copy of the assignment and assumption agreement, within fifteen (15) days of the effective date thereof.

**10.3 Key Person Provision.** Notwithstanding any percentage-of-interest threshold, Marcus J. Pellegrini shall serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer of Developer and shall exercise active and material day-to-day management authority over the Project. If Marcus J. Pellegrini ceases to serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer, Developer shall, within sixty (60) days, appoint a successor Manager reasonably acceptable to the City, and such cessation shall not constitute a default during such 60-day cure period.

**10.4 Construction Lender and Foreclosure Transfers.** Notwithstanding Section 10.1, Developer may assign this Agreement to a Construction Lender-designee or to a third-party purchaser at a foreclosure sale (or pursuant to a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure) without the City's prior consent; or, if City consent is required, such consent shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed, and the City shall respond within thirty (30) days of request. Any such assignee shall assume all of Developer's obligations under this Agreement.

**10.5 Notice of Purported Assignment.** Any purported assignment in violation of this Article 10 shall be void and of no force and effect and shall constitute a material default under this Agreement.

> **[Drafting Note — Change-of-control threshold conflict (critical open item).** Three different ownership thresholds appear across the source documents: (i) the Term Sheet (Section 10) permits assignment without City consent to an entity in which Pellegrini holds "at least a 50% controlling interest"; (ii) the Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Section 8.1) defines a Change of Control if Pellegrini ceases to hold at least [50]% (bracketed, subject to negotiation) and if any single investor/group acquires 25% or more; and (iii) the Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 5.2) defines a Change of Control if the "Pellegrini Interest" falls below 51%, if Pellegrini ceases as Manager/CEO (without replacement within 60 days), if any single person/entity (other than the existing members) acquires 25% or more, or upon a merger/consolidation/acquisition. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 5.3) acknowledges that the anticipated $58.4 million in additional financing for later phases may dilute the Pellegrini Interest below both 51% and 50%, and commits the parties to negotiate Development Agreement assignment/change-of-control provisions that include (i) carve-outs for internal transfers among existing members, (ii) carve-outs for capital raises approved by both Investor and the City, (iii) a "Key Person" provision as an alternative/supplement to rigid percentage thresholds, and (iv) a reasonable cure period (not less than 60 days). Section 10 reflects these recommendations: it replaces rigid percentage thresholds with a Key Person approach (Section 10.3), broadens Permitted Transfers (Section 10.2), and provides a 60-day cure period. The parties must harmonize the 50% (Term Sheet), [50%] (Lender, negotiable), and 51% (Lakeshore) thresholds. Developer's position is that the Key Person approach, combined with the broadened Permitted Transfers, best accommodates the anticipated future capital needs while preserving the City's interest in Pellegrini's continued leadership. Confirm the City's and the Construction Lender's agreement. **]**

> **[Drafting Note — Coordination with Construction Lender covenants (open item).** The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Section 8.1) provides that any dilution of the Pellegrini Family Trust's interest below [50]% requires the Lender's prior written consent and may trigger a Change of Control under the loan. The Development Agreement's assignment provisions and the Loan Documents must not conflict. Section 10.2(e) and the Key Person approach are intended to harmonize with the Lender's covenants, but the [50]% Lender threshold remains subject to negotiation. Confirm alignment. **]**

## ARTICLE 11. COMPLETION ASSURANCE (IN LIEU OF REVERSIONARY INTEREST)

**11.1 Fee Simple Absolute Title.** Developer shall hold fee simple absolute title to the Property, free and clear of any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, fee simple determinable, fee simple subject to condition subsequent, or any other form of defeasible fee estate in favor of the City or any other party. The City acknowledges that any such defeasible interest would render Developer's title uninsurable under standard ALTA title insurance policies, would subordinate the Construction Lender's first-priority mortgage, and would prevent the Project from being financed.

**11.2 Completion Assurance Mechanism.** In lieu of the reversionary interest contemplated by Resolution Condition No. 8, the parties agree to the following completion-assurance mechanism, which is designed to satisfy the City's legitimate interest in ensuring timely Phase I completion while preserving Developer's fee simple absolute title and the Construction Lender's first-priority security interest:

**(a) Liquidated Damages.** If Developer fails to achieve Substantial Completion of Phase I by the Phase I Substantial Completion Deadline (September 30, 2028), as such deadline may be extended by Force Majeure or other provisions of this Agreement, and such failure continues uncured after the expiration of all applicable cure periods (including the Construction Lender's extended cure period under Section 14.2), Developer shall pay to the City liquidated damages in the amount of [•] per [month/day] of delay, representing a reasonable pre-estimate of the City's actual damages resulting from the delay. Such liquidated damages shall constitute an unsecured contractual obligation of Developer and shall not constitute a lien on the Property.

**(b) City Repurchase Option.** As an alternative or supplement to the liquidated damages in Section 11.2(a), the City shall have a contractual option to repurchase the Property, exercisable only upon (i) Developer's failure to achieve Substantial Completion of Phase I within the agreed timeframe, (ii) expiration of all cure periods (including the Construction Lender's extended cure period), and (iii) the Construction Lender's prior written consent or full satisfaction of the outstanding construction loan balance. The repurchase price shall be the lesser of the Purchase Price paid by Developer or the then-current fair market value, as determined by an independent MAI appraiser.

**(c) Enhanced Completion Guaranty.** Developer shall cause Marcus J. Pellegrini and/or the Pellegrini Family Trust to provide an enhanced completion guaranty to the City, guaranteeing completion of Phase I within the agreed timeframe and providing the City with a direct contractual remedy against the guarantors (and their assets, other than the Property) in the event of a completion failure.

> **[Drafting Note — Reversionary interest vs. Lender prohibition (critical — central open item).** This is the most significant cross-document conflict. Resolution Condition No. 8 requires Developer to grant the City a reversionary interest in the Property, with fee simple title automatically reverting to the City (together with all improvements) if Phase I is not Substantially Complete within 36 months of conveyance, and requires the reversionary interest to be recorded against the Property at conveyance. The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Sections 4.2, 7.1, 9, and 12) expressly and repeatedly prohibits any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, or defeasible fee, stating that incorporation of such an interest "would make the Loan impossible to close" because it would (i) be uninsurable under ALTA title policies, (ii) subordinate the Lender's mortgage to the City's right to reclaim the property, (iii) prevent foreclosure, and (iv) render the loan unsecurable. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 7(d)) requires Investor consent to any clawback, reversionary interest, right of re-entry, repurchase option, or similar mechanism. Section 11 therefore eliminates the reversionary interest and substitutes alternative mechanisms (liquidated damages, a City repurchase option exercisable only after the Lender's mortgage is satisfied, and an enhanced completion guaranty) consistent with the alternatives suggested by the Construction Lender. The City must agree to substitute these mechanisms for the reversionary interest; without such agreement, the construction loan cannot close and the Project cannot proceed. **]**

> **[Drafting Note — 36-month reversionary window vs. September 30, 2028 deadline (critical).** Even if a reversionary interest were retained, the Resolution's 36-month window is measured from the date of conveyance, not from groundbreaking. If Closing occurs in mid-2025 (e.g., June 2025), the 36-month window would expire in approximately June 2028 — three months before the Phase I Substantial Completion Deadline of September 30, 2028. The reversionary interest would therefore trigger before the agreed completion deadline, an internal inconsistency. The substitute mechanisms in Section 11 are measured from the September 30, 2028 deadline (as extended), resolving this inconsistency. **]**

> **[Drafting Note — Selection among alternatives (open item).** The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Section 12) suggests five alternative clawback mechanisms: (i) contractual repurchase option (only after Lender's mortgage satisfied), (ii) performance escrow/additional letter of credit, (iii) liquidated damages, (iv) enhanced completion guaranty, and (v) right of first refusal. Section 11 incorporates (i), (iii), and (iv). The parties should select the combination acceptable to both the City and the Construction Lender. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 7(d)) requires Investor consent to the selected mechanism. **]**

## ARTICLE 12. DEFAULT, CURE, AND TERMINATION

**12.1 Monetary Defaults.** In the event of a monetary default by either party (including Developer's failure to pay the Purchase Price or the City's failure to fund committed infrastructure amounts), the non-defaulting party shall provide written notice specifying the nature and amount of the default. The defaulting party shall have sixty (60) days from receipt of such notice to cure the default.

**12.2 Non-Monetary Defaults.** In the event of a non-monetary default by either party, the non-defaulting party shall provide written notice specifying the nature of the default in reasonable detail. The defaulting party shall have ninety (90) days from receipt of such notice to cure the default; provided, however, that if the nature of the default is such that it cannot reasonably be cured within ninety (90) days, the defaulting party shall have such additional time as is reasonably necessary to effect a cure, so long as the defaulting party has commenced cure efforts within the initial 90-day period and is diligently and continuously pursuing cure to completion.

**12.3 Termination by City.** The City shall have the right to terminate this Agreement upon: (a) a material breach by Developer that remains uncured after the expiration of the applicable cure period; (b) Developer's abandonment of the Project, evidenced by cessation of all construction activity for a period of one hundred eighty (180) consecutive days (other than cessation caused by Force Majeure events); and (c) such other events of default as may be specified in this Agreement.

**12.4 Termination by Developer.** Developer shall have the right to terminate this Agreement upon: (a) the City's failure to deliver clean, marketable, and insurable title to the Property at Closing; (b) the City's failure to fund its committed infrastructure obligations ($12,500,000) within the timelines specified in Section 5.3; (c) a condemnation or taking by eminent domain (by any entity other than the City) of more than fifteen percent (15%) of the Property; (d) the City's failure to complete the rezoning of the Property to PD-TOD within the timeframe required for Developer to meet the Phase I groundbreaking deadline; and (e) such other events of default as may be specified in this Agreement.

> **[Drafting Note — City termination triggers (open item).** The Term Sheet (Section 15) permits the City to terminate if Developer fails to commence Phase I construction within six (6) months following land conveyance. As noted in the drafting note to Section 4.2, this six-month trigger conflicts with the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking deadline and has been omitted from Section 12.3. Confirm the City's agreement. The Term Sheet also lists "Developer's failure to commence Phase I construction within 6 months following land conveyance" as a City termination ground; this draft replaces it with the abandonment standard (180 consecutive days of no construction activity). **]**

**12.5 Remedies.** Upon termination of this Agreement by a non-defaulting party, the non-defaulting party shall have all remedies available at law or in equity, including damages, specific performance, and injunctive relief, subject in all cases to the dispute resolution provisions of Article 17. Neither party shall be liable to the other for consequential, special, or punitive damages, except as may be expressly provided herein or required by law.

**12.6 TIF Shortfall Not a Default.** A shortfall in TIF Reimbursement (including the inability to reach the $48,000,000 cap due to insufficient tax increment generation or TIF District expiration) shall not constitute a default by the City under this Agreement; provided, however, that the City shall comply with its good-faith obligations under Section 5.2(d).

> **[Drafting Note — TIF shortfall as default (open item).** Corporation Counsel's TIF District Summary Memorandum (Section VIII, recommendation 1(d)) recommends that the development agreement expressly state that a TIF shortfall does not constitute a City default. Section 12.6 adopts this provision. From the Developer's perspective, while the Developer accepts that TIF is not a guaranteed City obligation, the Developer's ability to complete later phases may depend on TIF performance. The parties should consider whether Developer's obligations to construct Phases II, III, and IV should be conditioned on TIF performance or whether Developer retains discretion to defer later phases. **]**

## ARTICLE 13. INDEMNIFICATION

**13.1 Mutual Indemnification.** Each party shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the other party, and its officers, employees, agents, and representatives, from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs) arising from or related to the indemnifying party's negligent acts or omissions, reckless conduct, or willful misconduct in connection with the performance of its obligations under this Agreement.

**13.2 Developer Indemnification of City.** Developer shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the City from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to construction activities on the Property, including without limitation claims for personal injury, death, property damage, and workers' compensation, regardless of whether such claims are brought by Developer's employees, contractors, subcontractors, or third parties.

**13.3 City Environmental Indemnification.** The City's indemnification of Developer for pre-existing environmental conditions is set forth in Section 6.4.

**13.4 Survival.** The indemnification obligations set forth in this Article 13 and in Section 6.4 shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement.

**13.5 Prevailing Wage Indemnification.** Developer shall indemnify the City for any claims, penalties, or liabilities arising from prevailing wage violations on the Project to the extent attributable to Developer's or its contractors' non-compliance with the PWA as applied under Section 9.1.

> **[Drafting Note — Prevailing wage indemnity scope (open item).** Corporation Counsel's TIF District Summary Memorandum (Section V.C) recommends a Developer indemnification of the City for prevailing wage violations and specified remedies including withholding of TIF reimbursement. Section 13.5 incorporates the indemnity. The scope of the indemnity should track the scope of prevailing wage applicability in Section 9.1 (i.e., limited to "public works" components if the narrower formulation is adopted). If the City insists on the broader "all construction" formulation, the indemnity scope would expand accordingly. Confirm. **]**

---

## ARTICLE 14. CONSTRUCTION LENDER PROTECTIONS

**14.1 Lender Notice.** The City shall provide the Construction Lender with prompt written notice of any default by Developer under this Agreement, concurrently with notice to Developer.

**14.2 Lender Cure Rights.** The Construction Lender shall have the right (but not the obligation) to cure any default by Developer under this Agreement. The Construction Lender's additional cure period shall be not less than thirty (30) days beyond Developer's cure period for monetary defaults and not less than sixty (60) days beyond Developer's cure period for non-monetary defaults, with such periods extended as necessary if the Construction Lender is diligently pursuing a cure that requires additional time (including time necessary to obtain possession of the Property through foreclosure or appointment of a receiver).

**14.3 Lender Consent to Amendments.** No material amendment, modification, supplement, or termination of this Agreement shall be effective without the Construction Lender's prior written consent, which shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed.

**14.4 Subordination of City Interests.** Any lien, encumbrance, or security interest that the City may hold or acquire in the Property (other than the completion-assurance mechanisms in Section 11, which are structured as contractual/financial claims rather than liens) shall be subordinated to the Construction Lender's mortgage pursuant to a subordination and intercreditor agreement in form and substance acceptable to the Construction Lender.

**14.5 Estoppel Certificates.** The City shall deliver estoppel certificates to the Construction Lender upon reasonable request (not more than twice per calendar year), certifying the status of this Agreement, the existence or absence of any outstanding defaults, and such other customary matters as the Construction Lender may reasonably request.

**14.6 Lender Review of Agreement.** This Agreement shall be delivered to the Construction Lender in draft form not less than fifteen (15) business days prior to its proposed execution date and shall not be executed until the Construction Lender has confirmed in writing that it is in form and substance acceptable to the Construction Lender.

> **[Drafting Note — Lender-required provisions (open item).** The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Section 12) requires the Development Agreement to include: (a) no reversionary interest (addressed in Section 11); (b) Lender cure rights (Section 14.2); (c) Lender consent to amendments (Section 14.3); (d) subordination of City interests (Section 14.4); (e) foreclosure/deed-in-lieu assignment (Section 10.4); and (f) estoppel certificates (Section 14.5). Article 14 incorporates these requirements, which are necessary for the construction loan to close. Confirm the City's agreement to each. **]**

## ARTICLE 15. REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES

**15.1 Developer Representations.** Developer represents and warrants to the City as of the Effective Date and as of Closing:

**(a)** Developer is a limited liability company duly organized, validly existing, and in good standing under the laws of the State of Illinois, with full power and authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform its obligations hereunder;

**(b)** The execution and delivery of this Agreement has been duly authorized by all necessary action and will not conflict with or violate Developer's organizational documents or any other material agreement to which Developer is a party;

**(c)** The ownership structure of Developer is as follows: Pellegrini Family Trust — 60%; Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP — 25%; TransitWorks Equity Group LLC — 15%;

**(d)** Developer has the financial capacity, development experience, and organizational resources necessary to complete the Project, with an entity-level net worth of approximately $185,000,000 (audited by Clearmont Accounting Group LLP) and bonding capacity of up to $75,000,000 through Ridgepoint Surety Corporation;

**(e)** Developer has committed equity of not less than $85,000,000 ($51,000,000 from Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP and $34,000,000 from the Pellegrini Family Trust); and

**(f)** There are no environmental conditions on the Property other than those disclosed in the Phase II ESA.

**15.2 City Representations.** The City represents and warrants to Developer as of the Effective Date and as of Closing:

**(a)** The City is a home rule municipality duly organized and existing under the laws of the State of Illinois, with full power and authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform its obligations hereunder;

**(b)** The execution and delivery of this Agreement has been duly authorized by all necessary action, including the adoption of Resolution No. R-2025-017, and approval by the City Council;

**(c)** The City has good and marketable fee simple title to the Property, free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, and adverse claims (except Permitted Exceptions), and has the legal authority to convey the Property to Developer;

**(d)** The Property is located within the Lakemont Central TIF District, and TIF funds are legally available for the purposes described in this Agreement, subject to annual appropriation and the availability of increment; and

**(e)** The City has disclosed all known environmental conditions on the Property, as reflected in the Phase II ESA.

The City's title representations should be supported by title commitments for each parcel. Confirm. Also reconcile the acquisition-history discrepancy flagged in the drafting note to Section 2.1. **]**

## ARTICLE 16. CONDITIONS PRECEDENT

**16.1 Conditions Precedent to Closing.** The obligation of each party to close the conveyance of the Property shall be subject to the satisfaction (or waiver by the applicable party) of the following conditions precedent:

**(a)** Execution of this Agreement by both parties and approval by the City Council;

**(b)** Completion of the rezoning of the Property from M-2 to PD-TOD, adopted by ordinance and effective, with the applicable appeal period expired without the filing of any appeal;

**(c)** Completion or authorization of parcel consolidation of the three parcels into a single parcel of record;

**(g)** Issuance of an ALTA lender's title insurance policy and a current ALTA/NSPS land title survey acceptable to the Construction Lender;

**(h)** An updated MAI appraisal demonstrating compliance with the Construction Lender's loan-to-value and loan-to-cost requirements;

**(i)** Environmental clearance for Parcel 12-24-300-016 (either an NFR Letter or VSRP enrollment with an approved RAP and an environmental escrow/insurance product satisfactory to the Construction Lender) and evidence that the engineered barrier for Parcel 12-24-300-017 is included in the Phase I construction budget;

**(j)** All insurance policies required under Section 9.3, with certificates and endorsements delivered;

**(k)** All necessary building permits and governmental approvals for Phase I construction activities;

**(l)** An executed GMP or stipulated-sum construction contract with a general contractor approved by the Construction Lender;

**(m)** Confirmation from the Construction Lender that this Agreement is in form acceptable to the lender; and

**(n)** Such other conditions as may be set forth in Exhibit [•].

> **[Drafting Note — Equity demonstration deadline conflict (open item).** Resolution Condition No. 1 requires Developer to demonstrate committed equity of not less than $85,000,000 within sixty (60) days of the adoption of the Resolution (i.e., on or before April 19, 2025). **16.2 Conditions Precedent to Each Phase.** The obligation of Developer to commence each subsequent phase shall be subject to: (a) no uncured default by the City; (b) the City's completion of off-site infrastructure necessary to support such phase; (c) Developer's securing of the Additional Financing or construction loan availability for such phase; and (d) such other conditions as may be set forth in Exhibit [•].

## ARTICLE 17. DISPUTE RESOLUTION

**17.1 Negotiation.** The parties shall first attempt to resolve any dispute arising under or related to this Agreement through good-faith negotiation between the City Manager (or designee) and Developer's CEO (or designee) for a period of thirty (30) days following written notice of the dispute.

**17.2 Mediation.** If the dispute is not resolved through negotiation within the 30-day period, the dispute shall be submitted to non-binding mediation before a neutral mediator mutually agreed upon (or the Lakemont Mediation Center). The costs of mediation shall be shared equally by the parties, and each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees.

**17.3 Binding Arbitration.** If the dispute is not resolved through mediation within sixty (60) days following submission to the mediator, the dispute shall be submitted to final and binding arbitration administered under the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association, conducted by a single arbitrator with experience in real estate development matters. The venue for any mediation or arbitration shall be Cook County, Illinois.

**17.4 Provisional Remedies.** Nothing in this Article 17 shall prevent either party from seeking temporary restraining orders, preliminary injunctions, or other provisional judicial relief where necessary to prevent irreparable harm, pending resolution of the dispute through the procedures set forth above.

**17.5 Governing Law.** This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Illinois, without regard to conflict-of-laws principles.

Vasquez, Director, conducted a competitive Request for Proposals process for the redevelopment of the Property as a mixed-use transit-oriented development, and selected Developer as the preferred developer based upon its qualifications, experience, project proposal, and demonstrated financial capacity;

> **[Drafting Note — RFP number conflict.** The Negotiated Term Sheet references "RFP No. 2024-CD-018," while the Developer's RFP Response Narrative references "RFP No. 2024-CD-07." Confirm the correct RFP number and conform all references. **]**

**WHEREAS**, Developer and the City executed a non-binding Negotiated Term Sheet dated January 22, 2025 (the "**Term Sheet**"), setting forth the principal economic and legal terms proposed to govern this Agreement;

**WHEREAS**, on February 18, 2025, the City Council of the City of Lakemont adopted Resolution No. R-2025-017 (the "**Resolution**"), granting conditional approval for the Project and authorizing the City Manager to negotiate and execute this Agreement, subject to the conditions set forth in Section 3 of the Resolution;

**WHEREAS**, the Property is currently zoned M-2 (General Manufacturing) and requires rezoning to PD-TOD (Planned Development–Transit Oriented District) to permit the proposed development program;

**WHEREAS**, the Property is located within the Lakemont Central Tax Increment Financing District, established by Ordinance No. 2019-42, expiring December 31, 2042 (the "**TIF District**");

**WHEREAS**, a Phase II Environmental Site Assessment was completed by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC in a report dated November 8, 2024 (the "**Phase II ESA**"), identifying certain environmental conditions on the Property requiring remediation;

**WHEREAS**, Developer has obtained a preliminary (non-binding) commitment from First Prairie National Bank (the "**Construction Lender**") for a construction loan facility in an amount of up to Two Hundred Ten Million Dollars ($210,000,000), subject to the execution of this Agreement in form and substance acceptable to the Construction Lender, and Developer's equity sources have committed a total of Eighty-Five Million Dollars ($85,000,000) in equity; and

**WHEREAS**, the City Council has determined that the Project will advance the public interest, health, safety, and welfare of the residents of the City by creating transit-oriented housing and commercial space, generating tax revenues, creating employment, providing affordable housing, remediating environmental contamination, and constructing public infrastructure and amenities.

**NOW, THEREFORE**, in consideration of the mutual covenants and agreements set forth herein, and other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the parties agree as follows:

## ARTICLE 1. DEFINITIONS

**1.1 Defined Terms.** In addition to terms defined elsewhere in this Agreement, the following capitalized terms shall have the meanings set forth below:

"**Additional Financing**" means the additional debt, mezzanine financing, and/or equity (estimated at approximately Fifty-Eight Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars ($58,400,000)) required to fund Phases III and IV, to be secured by Developer prior to commencement of each such phase.

"**Affordable Unit**" means a residential dwelling unit restricted to occupancy by households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of Area Median Income, as more particularly described in Section 8.1.

"**Agreement**" means this Development Agreement, including all Recitals, Articles, Sections, and Exhibits.

"**AMI**" means Area Median Income as published annually by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development for the Chicago-Naperville-Elgin, IL-IN-WI Metropolitan Statistical Area, or any successor designation.

"**Appraisal**" means the appraisal of the Property prepared by Crossland Appraisal Services, dated January 15, 2025, establishing an as-is fair market value of Eighteen Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars ($18,400,000).

"**Construction Lender**" means First Prairie National Bank, and any successor construction lender approved in accordance with Section 10.3.

"**Construction Loan Documents**" means the definitive loan agreements, mortgage, security instruments, guaranties, and other documentation evidencing the construction loan facility from the Construction Lender.

"**ELUC**" means an environmental land use control, in the form of a restrictive covenant, recorded against the Property in connection with the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program, as more particularly described in Section 6.2.

"**Force Majeure**" has the meaning set forth in Section 4.4.

"**IEPA**" means the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency.

"**NFR Letter**" means a No Further Remediation letter issued by the IEPA under the Voluntary Site Remediation Program.

"**Permitted Exceptions**" means the exceptions to title set forth in Exhibit [•], including: (i) real estate taxes not yet due and payable; (ii) standard utility and access easements not materially interfering with the Project; (iii) this Agreement; (iv) covenants and restrictions of the PD-TOD zoning ordinance; (v) environmental use controls or institutional controls recorded in connection with the IEPA VSRP NFR Letter and engineered barrier requirements, in each case as reviewed and approved by the Construction Lender's counsel; and (vi) such other exceptions as the Construction Lender may approve in writing.

"**Phase I**," "**Phase II**," "**Phase III**," and "**Phase IV**" mean the four phases of the Project as described in Section 3.1 and Exhibit [•] (Phasing Plan).

"**Project**" means the Lakemont Station Commons mixed-use transit-oriented development described in Article 3.

"**Property**" means the real property described in Section 2.1.

"**RAP**" means a Remedial Action Plan for the environmental remediation of Parcel 12-24-300-016, as more particularly described in Section 6.2.

"**Substantial Completion**" means, with respect to a phase of the Project, the completion of construction of such phase to the point where the building or improvements can be occupied and used for their intended purpose, as evidenced by the issuance of a temporary or final certificate of occupancy by the City's Building Department.

"**TIF Act**" means the Illinois Tax Increment Allocation Redevelopment Act, 65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-1 *et seq.*

"**TIF Reimbursement**" means the reimbursement of eligible redevelopment project costs from tax increment revenues generated within the TIF District, as more particularly described in Section 5.2.

"**VSRP**" means the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program.

**1.2 Incorporation of Recitals.** The Recitals set forth above are incorporated into and made a part of this Agreement as though fully set forth herein.

## ARTICLE 2. THE PROPERTY AND LAND CONVEYANCE

**2.1 The Property.** The Property consists of three (3) parcels of record located at 1400–1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, Illinois 60048, identified by Parcel Identification Numbers 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017, totaling approximately 12.7 acres (553,212 square feet), to be consolidated into a single parcel of record prior to or concurrent with conveyance to Developer, as more particularly described in the legal description attached as Exhibit [•].

> **[Drafting Note — Parcel acquisition history conflict.** The Resolution and the TIF District Summary Memorandum state that the City acquired Parcels 12-24-300-015 and 12-24-300-016 through tax forfeiture proceedings in 2016, and Parcel 12-24-300-017 by direct purchase in 2018. The Phase II ESA, however, states that Parcel 12-24-300-016 was acquired by the City through direct purchase in 2018 (together with Parcel 017), and that Parcel 12-24-300-015 was acquired through tax forfeiture in 2016. This discrepancy affects (i) the chain of title and encumbrances applicable to each parcel, (ii) the scope of the City's indemnification for pre-existing environmental conditions, and (iii) the City's responsible-party status under the Illinois Environmental Protection Act. Confirm the correct acquisition history for each parcel and conform the recitals and the City's representations in Section 15.2 accordingly. **]**

> **[Drafting Note — Parcel acreage conflict.** The Phase II ESA reports individual parcel sizes of approximately 4.2 acres (015), 4.1 acres (016), and 4.4 acres (017), while the RFP Response Narrative reports 3.8 acres (015), 4.1 acres (016), and 4.8 acres (017). The total of approximately 12.7 acres is consistent. Confirm the legal descriptions and exact acreages from the survey and consolidation plat. **]**

**2.2 Conveyance; Purchase Price.** Upon the satisfaction or waiver of all conditions precedent set forth in Article 16, the City shall convey the Property to Developer by special warranty deed, free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, restrictions, reversionary interests, rights of re-entry, possibilities of reverter, defeasible conditions, and other title defects or exceptions, except for the Permitted Exceptions. The purchase price for the Property shall be Nine Million Two Hundred Thousand Dollars ($9,200,000) (the "**Purchase Price**"), representing fifty percent (50%) of the appraised as-is fair market value of Eighteen Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars ($18,400,000) established in the Appraisal. The Purchase Price shall be due and payable at closing. Developer may fund the Purchase Price from equity, construction loan proceeds, or a combination thereof.

> **[Drafting Note — Reversionary interest prohibition (critical).** The Resolution, Condition No. 8, requires Developer to grant the City a reversionary interest in the Property if Phase I is not Substantially Complete within 36 months of conveyance. The Construction Lender's Term Sheet expressly prohibits any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, or defeasible fee estate, stating that incorporation of such an interest "would make the Loan impossible to close." Section 2.2 and Section 11 of this draft therefore provide for fee simple absolute title and substitute an alternative completion-assurance mechanism (see Section 11). This is the central open item and must be resolved with the City before the Construction Lender will commit. The Lakeshore Side Letter, Section 7(d), also requires Investor consent to any clawback mechanism. **]**

**2.3 Title Insurance.** The City shall deliver marketable and insurable fee simple title to the Property and shall provide, at the City's cost, a standard owner's title insurance policy in the amount of the Purchase Price issued by a nationally recognized title insurance company. Developer (and the Construction Lender) shall receive an ALTA lender's title insurance policy in the amount of the construction loan, with such endorsements as the Construction Lender may require, including zoning, comprehensive, survey, access, contiguity, planned development, environmental lien, and construction loan endorsements.

**2.4 Closing.** Closing of the conveyance of the Property (the "**Closing**") shall occur within thirty (30) days following the satisfaction or waiver of all conditions precedent set forth in Article 16. The parties' target Closing date is [mid-2025], subject to the timing of rezoning, IEPA approvals, and the satisfaction of other conditions.

**2.5 Phased Conveyance.** Notwithstanding the foregoing, the parties may agree to a phased conveyance of the Property, pursuant which Parcel 12-24-300-015 (which requires no remediation) and Parcel 12-24-300-017 (which requires only the engineered barrier) may be conveyed to Developer prior to completion of the remediation of Parcel 12-24-300-016, in order to permit early commencement of construction on the clean portions of the Property while remediation of Parcel 12-24-300-016 proceeds concurrently.

> **[Drafting Note — Phased conveyance vs. Resolution Condition No. 2 (open item).** Resolution Condition No. 2 requires that, "prior to any conveyance of the Property or any portion thereof to the Developer," the Developer shall have submitted and the IEPA shall have approved a RAP for Parcel 12-24-300-016. The Phase II ESA recommends a phased conveyance approach (conveying clean Parcels 015 and 017 first) to allow Phase I construction to begin on clean parcels while Parcel 016 remediation proceeds, noting that this could reduce project-wide delay risk. The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Section 7.1, condition 7) accepts, for Parcel 016, either (a) an NFR Letter or (b) VSRP enrollment with an approved RAP plus an environmental escrow/insurance product. The parties should confirm whether the Resolution's "prior to any conveyance" language requires RAP approval before conveyance of the clean parcels, or only before conveyance of Parcel 016, and whether a phased conveyance is compatible with the PD-TOD rezoning and parcel consolidation requirements. Developer's preference is phased conveyance to protect the Phase I groundbreaking deadline. **]**

## ARTICLE 3. THE PROJECT AND DEVELOPMENT PROGRAM

**3.1 The Project.** Developer shall design, entitle, construct, and deliver a mixed-use transit-oriented development known as "Lakemont Station Commons" (the "**Project**"), with a total gross building area of approximately 1,620,000 square feet across four phases, comprising the following program elements:

| Component | Phase I | Phase II | Phase III | Phase IV | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Residential Units | 320 (256 market + 64 affordable) | — | 210 (168 market + 42 affordable) | — | 530 |
| Affordable Units (≤60% AMI) | 64 | — | 42 | — | 106 (20%) |
| Office (sq ft) | — | 280,000 | — | — | 280,000 |
| Retail (sq ft) | 45,000 | 18,000 | — | — | 63,000 |
| Hotel (rooms) | — | — | 150 | — | 150 |
| Community Center (sq ft) | — | — | — | 28,000 | 28,000 |
| Public Plaza (acres) | — | — | — | 2.5 | 2.5 |
| Parking (spaces) | 480 | 350 | — | 600 | 1,430 |
| Gross Building Area (sq ft) | 420,000 | 380,000 | 440,000 | 380,000 | 1,620,000 |
| Estimated Hard Construction Cost | $105,000,000 | $76,000,000 | $88,000,000 | $71,000,000 | $340,000,000 |

The total estimated Project cost, inclusive of soft costs, land, financing, environmental remediation, and infrastructure, is approximately Four Hundred Twelve Million Dollars ($412,000,000). The detailed development program, including site plan, phasing plan, and project budget, is set forth in Exhibit [•].

> **[Drafting Note — Affordable unit count conflict.** The Resolution (Condition No. 7) and the RFP Response Narrative require not less than twenty percent (20%) of total residential units to be affordable, which equals 106 units. The Term Sheet's summary table (Section 3.5) references "at least 100 affordable units," while its detailed breakdown (Section 12) confirms 106 units. The 20% / 106-unit figure is the binding requirement. This draft uses 106 units. Confirm that the City accepts 106 as the fixed obligation (not a higher number) and that the "at least 100" language in the Term Sheet summary is superseded. **]**

**3.2 Phase Descriptions.** The four phases of the Project shall be as follows:

**(a) Phase I — Residential Tower A and Retail Podium.** Three hundred twenty (320) residential units (256 market-rate and 64 affordable), 45,000 square feet of ground-floor retail, and a 480-space structured parking facility; approximately 420,000 square feet of gross building area; estimated hard construction cost of $105,000,000.

**(b) Phase II — Office Building and Retail.** Two hundred eighty thousand (280,000) square feet of Class A office space, 18,000 square feet of retail, and a 350-space structured parking facility; approximately 380,000 square feet of gross building area; estimated hard construction cost of $76,000,000.

**(c) Phase III — Residential Tower B and Hotel.** Two hundred ten (210) residential units (168 market-rate and 42 affordable) and a 150-room select-service hotel; approximately 440,000 square feet of gross building area; estimated hard construction cost of $88,000,000.

**(d) Phase IV — Public Plaza, Community Center, and Parking Structure.** A 2.5-acre public plaza, a 28,000-square-foot community center (to be conveyed to the City upon completion), and a 600-space public parking structure; approximately 380,000 square feet of gross building area; estimated hard construction cost of $71,000,000.

**3.3 Community Center Conveyance.** Upon Substantial Completion of Phase IV, Developer shall convey the community center to the City in fee simple, by special warranty deed, at no additional cost to the City. The community center shall be designed in consultation with the City's Parks and Recreation Department. The program, design specifications, and scope of furniture, fixtures, and equipment to be included in the conveyance shall be set forth in Exhibit [•].

> **[Drafting Note — Community center FF&E (open item).** The Term Sheet states the community center is to be conveyed "at no additional cost" to the City, while the RFP Response Narrative states it will be conveyed "fully furnished and equipped per the agreed-upon specifications." The scope and cost of furniture, fixtures, and equipment (FF&E) to be included in the conveyance, and whether such FF&E is TIF-eligible, must be defined and capped in Exhibit [•]. Developer's position: the conveyance obligation should be limited to the building shell and core systems plus a defined, capped FF&E package, with any FF&E beyond the defined scope at the City's expense. **]**

**3.4 Design Review.** Developer shall submit plans and specifications for each phase to the City's Community Development Department for design review and approval. The City shall review and respond to submissions within [thirty (30)] business days. The City's approval shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed. The City's review shall be limited to conformity with the PD-TOD zoning standards, this Agreement, and applicable codes, and shall not extend to aesthetic matters within Developer's reasonable design discretion except as expressly set forth in the PD-TOD ordinance.

## ARTICLE 4. PHASING AND CONSTRUCTION SCHEDULE

**4.1 Phasing Schedule.** Developer shall construct the Project in four phases in accordance with the following schedule:

| Phase | Commencement Deadline | Substantial Completion Deadline |
|---|---|---|
| Phase I | Groundbreaking no later than March 1, 2026 | September 30, 2028 |
| Phase II | Within 12 months of Phase I Substantial Completion | Within 24 months of Phase II commencement |
| Phase III | Within 12 months of Phase II Substantial Completion | Within 30 months of Phase III commencement |
| Phase IV | Concurrent with Phase III commencement | Within 24 months of Phase IV commencement |

**4.2 Phase I Commencement.** Phase I groundbreaking shall occur no later than March 1, 2026, provided that the City has (a) completed the rezoning of the Property to PD-TOD, (b) completed or authorized parcel consolidation, (c) conveyed the Property (or the applicable clean parcels, in the case of a phased conveyance) to Developer, and (d) commenced or completed the off-site infrastructure improvements necessary to support Phase I, in each case in accordance with the timelines set forth in this Agreement.

> **[Drafting Note — Phase I commencement window conflict (critical).** The Term Sheet (Section 15) permits the City to terminate the Agreement if Developer fails to commence Phase I construction within six (6) months following land conveyance. If Closing occurs in mid-2025 (e.g., June 2025), the six-month window would expire in December 2025 — approximately three months before the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking deadline. This draft deletes the six-month commencement trigger and ties commencement solely to the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking deadline, which is the operative milestone in both the Term Sheet and the Resolution. Confirm the City's agreement to remove the six-month trigger or to measure it from a date that does not precede the March 1, 2026 deadline. **]**

**4.3 Construction Progress Reports.** Developer shall provide quarterly construction progress reports to the City's Community Development Department, including percentage of completion, updated schedule, and identification of any delays or anticipated deviations, within thirty (30) days following the end of each calendar quarter during which active construction is occurring.

**4.4 Force Majeure.**

**(a) Definition.** "Force Majeure" means acts of God, fire, flood, earthquake, hurricane, tornado, severe weather events, pandemic, epidemic, quarantine, governmental health orders, war, terrorism, civil unrest, insurrection, strikes or labor disputes (other than those caused by Developer's labor practices or failure to comply with applicable labor laws), industry-wide supply chain disruptions beyond Developer's reasonable control, governmental moratoria on construction, and actions or inactions of governmental authorities (other than the City) that prevent or materially delay construction. Force Majeure shall not include financial inability to perform, changes in market conditions, increases in the cost of labor or materials (unless attributable to an industry-wide supply chain disruption), Developer's failure to obtain financing, or any event caused by Developer's negligence or willful misconduct.

**(b) Effect.** Force Majeure events shall extend the applicable deadlines for commencement and Substantial Completion on a day-for-day basis for the duration of the Force Majeure event.

**(c) Aggregate Cap.** The maximum extension of time available under this Section 4.4 shall not exceed eighteen (18) months in the aggregate across the Project.

**(d) Notice and Mitigation.** Developer shall provide written notice to the City within fifteen (15) business days of the occurrence of a Force Majeure event and shall use commercially reasonable efforts to mitigate its effects.

> **[Drafting Note — Force majeure cap conflict.** The Term Sheet provides an 18-month aggregate force majeure cap across the Project. The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (Section 9, condition 9) permits force majeure extensions of only up to 180 days (six months) for the Phase I Substantial Completion default. The Lender's shorter 180-day limit could cause a loan default even where the City has granted a longer extension under this Agreement. Developer should negotiate with the Construction Lender to align the force majeure periods, or structure the Lender's default to track the City-approved extensions. **]**

> **[Drafting Note — Remediation delay as force majeure (open item).** The Phase II ESA recommends that the transactional documents address whether delay in issuance of the NFR Letter for Parcel 12-24-300-016 (estimated 14–18 months, but potentially longer) constitutes a Force Majeure event, a basis for deadline extension, or a potential default. Given that the contamination is pre-existing and the remediation timeline is subject to IEPA review beyond Developer's control, Developer's position is that regulatory delays in NFR Letter issuance should constitute Force Majeure or an automatic deadline extension. Confirm. **]**

## ARTICLE 5. PUBLIC INCENTIVES

**5.1 Land Write-Down.** The conveyance of the Property at a Purchase Price of $9,200,000, representing 50% of the appraised fair market value, constitutes a land write-down incentive with a value of $9,200,000. The land write-down shall constitute a separate incentive and shall not be charged against, or reduce, the TIF Reimbursement cap set forth in Section 5.2.

> **[Drafting Note — Land write-down vs. TIF cap (open item).** The TIF District Summary Memorandum (Section VII) flags that, if the City records the land write-down as a TIF expenditure from the Special Tax Allocation Fund, it counts against the $48,000,000 TIF cap, and recommends the development agreement specify whether the write-down is charged against the cap or funded from another source. Developer's position, reflected in Section 5.1, is that the land write-down is a separate incentive funded from the City's general economic development resources and does not reduce the TIF cap. Confirm the City's agreement and the funding source. **]**

**5.2 Tax Increment Financing.**

**(a) TIF Reimbursement Commitment.** The City shall pledge up to Forty-Eight Million Dollars ($48,000,000) in tax increment revenues over the remaining life of the TIF District (expiring December 31, 2042) to reimburse Developer for eligible redevelopment project costs incurred in connection with the Project, including without limitation public infrastructure improvements, environmental remediation costs, parking structure construction costs, and other eligible project costs under the TIF Act and the Lakemont Central TIF District Redevelopment Plan adopted by Ordinance No. 2019-42.

**(b) Pay-As-You-Go.** TIF Reimbursement shall be on a pay-as-you-go basis. Developer shall front all eligible costs and shall be reimbursed annually from available tax increment as generated by the Project and other developments within the TIF District. The City shall not issue TIF bonds or notes for the Project.

**(c) Reimbursement Priority.** TIF Reimbursement shall be made in the following priority order: (i) first, environmental remediation costs for Parcels 12-24-300-016 and 12-24-300-017; (ii) second, Developer's eligible on-site infrastructure expenditures; (iii) third, public parking structure, public plaza, and community center costs associated with Phase IV; and (iv) fourth, all other eligible costs, including professional services and financing costs.

**(d) City Efforts to Maximize Reimbursement.** The City covenants to use good-faith efforts to maximize the tax increment available for TIF Reimbursement, including by seeking an extension of the TIF District or the establishment of a successor TIF district if, as of a benchmark date of December 31, 2038, cumulative TIF Reimbursement is tracking materially below the projections set forth in Exhibit [•].

**(e) No Reduction for Abatement.** The Phase I property tax abatement set forth in Section 5.4 shall not be structured or applied in a manner that reduces the tax increment available for TIF Reimbursement below the projections set forth in Exhibit [•] to the extent legally permissible.

> **[Drafting Note — TIF shortfall risk (critical open item).** The TIF District Summary Memorandum (Section IV) concludes that, under a base-case projection without abatement impact, cumulative increment reaches approximately $49.3 million by 2041 and $54.2 million by 2042 (exceeding the $48 million cap). However, under an adjusted scenario incorporating the Phase I property tax abatement, cumulative increment through 2042 is estimated at only approximately $38 million to $42 million — potentially $6 million to $10 million short of the $48 million cap. Corporation Counsel recommends that the development agreement (i) make TIF Reimbursement expressly contingent on actual increment collected and not a guaranteed City obligation, (ii) provide that a TIF shortfall does not constitute a City default, and (iii) include a City covenant to use good-faith efforts to seek a TIF extension. From the Developer's perspective, the Developer fronts all eligible costs and bears the timing and shortfall risk under a pay-as-you-go structure. Developer's positions reflected in this draft: (a) the reimbursement priority in Section 5.2(c) ensures Developer's most critical fronted costs (remediation and on-site infrastructure) are reimbursed first; (b) the City's good-faith extension covenant in Section 5.2(d); and (c) the abatement-no-reduction provision in Section 5.2(e). The parties should further negotiate whether the City will provide any backstop or alternative funding mechanism for a material TIF shortfall, and whether the Developer's obligation to construct later phases should be conditioned on TIF performance. The Lakeshore Side Letter (Section 4.3(a)) requires Investor consent to any change to the TIF reimbursement cap or eligible cost categories. **]**

> **[Drafting Note — Abatement-TIF interaction (open item).** Corporation Counsel notes an internal tension: the Phase I abatement benefits Developer by reducing its property tax burden but simultaneously reduces the TIF increment available for reimbursement. Corporation Counsel suggests structuring the abatement to apply only to the non-TIF-captured portion of the tax bill, if legally permissible, or obtaining an express acknowledgment that the abatement reduces TIF capacity. Section 5.2(e) adopts the former approach. Confirm legal feasibility with the City and outside counsel. **]**

**5.3 Infrastructure Cost-Sharing.**

**(a) City Off-Site Infrastructure.** The City shall fund Twelve Million Five Hundred Thousand Dollars ($12,500,000) for off-site infrastructure improvements, including the widening of Lakemont Station Boulevard, installation of a new traffic signal at the Oak Street intersection, extension of water main service, and upgrade of sanitary sewer capacity. The City's off-site infrastructure funding shall be appropriated from the City's general revenue or capital improvement funds and shall not be charged against, or reduce, the TIF Reimbursement cap.

**(b) Developer On-Site Infrastructure.** Developer shall fund Eight Million Three Hundred Thousand Dollars ($8,300,000) for on-site infrastructure improvements, including internal roadways, utilities, stormwater management, pedestrian pathways, and landscape improvements. Developer's eligible on-site infrastructure expenditures shall be reimbursable from TIF Reimbursement subject to the priority in Section 5.2(c) and Developer's substantiation of the public character of each component.

**(c) Total Infrastructure Budget.** The total combined infrastructure budget is Twenty Million Eight Hundred Thousand Dollars ($20,800,000).

**(d) City Infrastructure Timing.** The City's off-site infrastructure improvements shall be completed on a schedule coordinated with the Phase I construction timeline, and in any event the City shall complete the improvements necessary to support Phase I occupancy no later than [the date that is six (6) months prior to Phase I Substantial Completion]. The City's failure to fund or complete its committed infrastructure obligations within the timelines specified herein shall constitute a material default by the City and a basis for Developer termination under Section 12.3.

> **[Drafting Note — City infrastructure funding source (open item).** The TIF District Summary Memorandum (Section VII) flags that, if the City's $12.5 million infrastructure commitment is funded from TIF, it counts against the $48 million cap; if funded from general revenue or capital improvement funds, it does not. Section 5.3(a) provides for funding from general revenue/capital funds. Confirm the City's funding source. **]**

**5.4 Property Tax Abatement.** The City shall provide a twelve (12)-year declining property tax abatement on Phase I improvements only, as follows: Years 1–4, 75% abatement; Years 5–8, 50% abatement; Years 9–12, 25% abatement; Year 13 and thereafter, 0% (full assessment). The abatement shall apply to the incremental increase in assessed value attributable to the Phase I improvements, measured from the pre-development base equalized assessed value. No property tax abatement is provided for Phase II, Phase III, or Phase IV.

> **[Drafting Note — Abatement for later phases (open item).** The Term Sheet (Section 18.4) confirms that no abatement is provided for Phases II, III, or IV, and states that the parties' respective positions regarding the financial structure of later phases, including any additional incentives, shall be addressed during negotiation of the definitive Agreement. Developer may seek additional incentives for later phases; this is an open item. **]**

**5.5 Building Permit Fee Waiver.** The City shall waive building permit fees for Phase I and Phase II of the Project, with an estimated combined value of One Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars ($1,400,000) ($920,000 for Phase I and $480,000 for Phase II). No permit fee waiver is contemplated for Phase III or Phase IV. The permit fee waivers shall constitute a separate incentive and shall not be charged against the TIF Reimbursement cap.

**5.6 Summary of Public Incentives.** The public incentives are summarized as follows:

| Incentive | Value |
|---|---|
| Land Write-Down | $9,200,000 |
| TIF Reimbursement (up to) | $48,000,000 |
| City Off-Site Infrastructure Funding | $12,500,000 |
| Property Tax Abatement (Phase I, 12 years) | To be calculated based on actual assessed values |
| Permit Fee Waivers (Phases I & II) | $1,400,000 |
